proposals.title: Subcellular Pharmacokinetics of Metal-Based Anticancer Agents: Linking Organelle-Specific Accumulation to Synthetic Lethality and Aneuploidy
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of platinum drugs like cisplatin is critically dependent on nuclear DNA damage, yet their subcellular trafficking and sequestration in organelles like mitochondria or zinc-rich vesicles remains poorly mapped. This incomplete understanding limits our ability to predict drug response and design agents like phenanthriplatin that exploit alternative mechanisms. Pre-2026 work highlights the importance of mobile zinc in cellular signaling and the potential of synthetic lethality, but a systematic spatial analysis of metal-drug localization is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the differential potency of platinum and non-canonical metal-based agents is dictated by their specific subcellular localization patterns, which in turn modulate mechanisms of cell death, including the induction of synthetic lethality and aneuploidy.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-modal imaging and analytical platform. First, we will synthesize fluorescently tagged analogs of cisplatin, phenanthriplatin, and novel zinc-chelator conjugates. Their uptake and real-time localization will be tracked in live cancer cells using confocal microscopy co-stained with organelle-specific dyes (e.g., MitoTracker, LysoTracker). Second, we will use synchrotron-based X-ray fluorescence microscopy (XFM) to map the endogenous elemental (Zn, Ca, Pt) distributions at sub-100 nm resolution in fixed cells, correlating drug accumulation with native metal homeostasis. Third, we will integrate these spatial data with functional assays for DNA damage response, cell-cycle checkpoint activation, and chromosome segregation errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive 'subcellular pharmacokinetic' map for a class of metal-based therapeutics, directly linking drug localization to mechanistic outcomes. It will provide a rational framework for designing next-generation agents that target specific organelles to augment synthetic lethality or disrupt metal-mediated signaling pathways.
proposals.novelty: While uptake studies are common, systematically correlating high-resolution, element-specific localization of metal drugs with functional consequences like aneuploidy is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk cellular measurements to a spatially resolved understanding of drug action, integrating Lippard's expertise in zinc biology, synthetic lethality, and metallodrug design.

proposals.title: Structural Determinants of Cellular Lesion Formation: Integrating Platinum-DNA Adduct Structures with Cellular Zinc Homeostasis to Predict Chemotherapeutic Response
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of platinum anticancer drugs like cisplatin is determined by the formation of specific DNA lesions, but cellular responses vary widely. Pre-2026 work shows mobile zinc modulates DNA repair pathways and sensory perception, and synthetic lethality approaches can enhance platinum response. A critical gap exists in directly linking the structural features of DNA-bound platinum complexes to the downstream cellular lesions—such as replication stress, aneuploidy, and altered metal ion homeostasis—that ultimately drive cell death or resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the three-dimensional structure of platinum-DNA adducts, particularly those of cisplatin and phenanthriplatin, dictates specific cellular lesion profiles by differentially recruiting zinc-dependent proteins and disrupting metal ion homeostasis, which can be computationally predicted and experimentally validated.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution X-ray crystallography and molecular dynamics simulations to solve structures of key platinum-DNA adducts. These structural datasets will be integrated into a predictive computational pipeline using machine learning (specifically, graph neural networks trained on protein-DNA interaction databases) to map adduct features to probable cellular outcomes, including replication fork stalling and zinc transporter mislocalization. Predictions will be tested in cells using live-cell zinc imaging (e.g., with Zinpyr sensors), assays for aneuploidy, and measurements of drug uptake and synthetic lethality with DNA repair inhibitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind structure-to-function framework for platinum drugs, moving beyond adduct identification to predicting cellular sequelae. It will identify novel, zinc-dependent synthetic lethality targets to augment chemotherapy and provide a blueprint for designing next-generation metallotherapeutics based on predicted lesion profiles.
proposals.novelty: While structural studies of platinum-DNA adducts exist, their integration with dynamic cellular zinc biology and machine learning to predict therapeutic lesions is unprecedented. This approach uniquely bridges in vitro structural biology with in-cell metal ion signaling and phenotypic outcomes like aneuploidy.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Mapping of Platinum and Zinc Complex Metabolism in Cancer Cells Using Correlated Single-Cell Mass Cytometry and Organelle-Specific Fluorescent Probes
proposals.motivation: Understanding the intracellular distribution and metabolic fate of metallodrugs like cisplatin and zinc-targeting probes is crucial for elucidating mechanisms of action and resistance. Pre-2026, methods often provide bulk averages or lack organelle specificity, obscuring critical cell-to-cell heterogeneity and subcellular trafficking. This gap limits our ability to link metal complex localization to functional outcomes like synthetic lethality or aneuploidy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the differential subcellular localization and metabolism of platinum-based drugs (cisplatin, phenanthriplatin) and zinc-targeting agents are key determinants of their cytotoxicity and can be leveraged to create augmented synthetic lethality in cancer cells with DNA repair deficiencies.
proposals.method: We will employ high-dimensional single-cell mass cytometry (CyTOF) with a novel panel of metal-tagged antibodies and lanthanide-tagged small molecules to quantify the uptake and phenotypic impact of metal complexes across thousands of individual cells. This will be correlated with live-cell imaging using organelle-targeted, turn-on fluorescent sensors (e.g., for mobile zinc in the Golgi or mitochondria) and a recently reported traceless release probe for zinc-activated payloads. Data analysis will utilize viSNE and PhenoGraph algorithms to identify distinct cell fates linked to metal distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, correlated map of platinum drug metabolism and zinc flux at the single-cell and organelle level. It will directly link specific intracellular metal localization patterns to therapeutic outcomes like cell-cycle arrest and death, offering a new framework for designing combination therapies based on metal biology.
proposals.novelty: The integration of CyTOF for population-level metal complex fate with organelle-specific, dynamic zinc imaging in the same cellular systems is a novel, multi-modal approach. It moves beyond bulk uptake measurements to define how spatial and population heterogeneity in metal handling dictates chemotherapeutic response.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Tracking of Synthetic Platinum and Zinc Probes Reveals Compartment-Specific Uptake and Trafficking Dynamics in Live Cells
proposals.motivation: The cellular uptake and subcellular trafficking of metallodrugs like cisplatin and synthetic analogs (e.g., phenanthriplatin) are critical determinants of efficacy and resistance, yet these processes are poorly resolved in real time within live cells. Similarly, the dynamics of mobile zinc pools, which modulate stress responses and may influence chemotherapy outcomes, remain spatially and temporally opaque. Pre-2026, bulk measurements of uptake mask heterogeneous cellular behaviors and fail to capture the precise journey of individual metal complexes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-molecule fluorescence tracking will reveal distinct, real-time trafficking pathways for platinum-based chemotherapeutics and zinc probes, and that these pathways will correlate with cellular outcomes such as drug response and the induction of synthetic lethal vulnerabilities.
proposals.method: We will synthesize fluorescently tagged, biologically active derivatives of phenanthriplatin and zinc-selective probes (e.g., Zinpyr-type sensors). Using total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) and highly inclined and laminated optical sheet (HILO) microscopy, we will track individual molecules in live cancer cells. Trajectories will be analyzed using algorithms for single-particle tracking (e.g., u-track) and machine learning-based classification to identify distinct diffusion states (e.g., free, confined, directed) and localization to specific organelles, correlating dynamics with cellular phenotypes like aneuploidy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, real-time visualization of synthetic metallodrug and mobile zinc trafficking at the single-molecule level in live cells. It will establish a foundational methodology for studying metal complex cell biology and identify specific subcellular bottlenecks or reservoirs that govern drug efficacy. The findings could inform the design of next-generation metal-based agents with optimized delivery.
proposals.novelty: While single-molecule tracking is established for proteins, its application to directly visualize small-molecule synthetic metal complexes in real time is novel. Combining this with Lippard's expertise in zinc biology and platinum drug design creates a unique approach to link metal complex dynamics directly to chemotherapeutic mechanisms and synthetic lethality.

proposals.title: Computational Design of Zinc-Phenanthriplatin Hybrids for Selective Non-Covalent DNA Binding and Synthetic Lethality in Aneuploid Cancers
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin's efficacy is limited by covalent DNA damage resistance mechanisms, including nucleotide excision repair (NER). Phenanthriplatin, a cationic monofunctional platinum(II) complex, shows enhanced cellular uptake and a distinct, non-covalent-like interaction profile, but its design space is underexplored. Concurrently, mobile zinc is a potent endogenous signaling ion, and synthetic zinc complexes can catalyze bio-orthogonal reactions, suggesting a novel strategy for developing metallodrugs that exploit non-covalent DNA recognition and zinc biology.
proposals.hypothesis: Hybrid complexes combining a zinc-binding ligand framework with a phenanthriplatin-inspired monofunctional platinum center can be computationally designed to bind DNA through specific, non-covalent interactions, and their cellular activity will be augmented in aneuploid cancer cells via synthetic lethality with NER and cell-cycle checkpoint deficiencies.
proposals.method: We will employ molecular docking simulations using AutoDock Vina and the more rigorous hybrid quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics (QM/MM) protocol in Schrödinger's Maestro to screen designed libraries of zinc-phenanthriplatin hybrids against high-resolution DNA structures (e.g., minor groove, G-quadruplex). The zinc site will be parameterized using the bonded metal model in the OPLS4 forcefield. Top candidates will be synthesized, and their DNA binding mode (via fluorescence displacement, CD spectroscopy), cellular zinc mobilization (using FluoZin-3), uptake (by ICP-MS), and cytotoxicity in NER-deficient vs. proficient, aneuploid vs. diploid cell lines will be evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a computational pipeline for the rational design of non-covalent metallo-DNA binders, moving beyond classical covalent adduct formation. It will deliver new chemical tools to probe the interplay between zinc signaling, DNA recognition, and synthetic lethality, potentially identifying lead compounds with novel mechanisms to overcome platinum resistance.
proposals.novelty: While computational docking is standard for organic molecules, its rigorous application to design bifunctional metal complexes for deliberate non-covalent DNA targeting is novel. The strategy uniquely integrates Lippard lab expertise in zinc biology, monofunctional platinum agents, and synthetic lethality into a single drug design paradigm.

proposals.title: Proteomic and Metabolomic Fate Mapping of Synthetic Phenanthriplatin and Native Zinc Complexes Using Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: The efficacy and toxicity of platinum anticancer drugs like cisplatin are governed by their interactions with cellular biomolecules, but a comprehensive map of these interactions remains incomplete. Similarly, the biological roles of mobile zinc, including its recently discovered catalytic function in small molecule release, are mediated by transient, labile interactions with proteins and metabolites that are challenging to capture. A systematic, untargeted approach is needed to define the full spectrum of biomolecular targets for both synthetic metallodrugs and native metal ions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrated proteomic and metabolomic mass spectrometry will reveal distinct, high-value biomolecular interaction profiles for the synthetic platinum(IV) prodrug phenanthriplatin and for native zinc pools, identifying novel protein adducts and metal-altered metabolic pathways that dictate cellular response.
proposals.method: We will employ quantitative, label-free LC-MS/MS proteomics (using a Q Exactive HF mass spectrometer and MaxQuant/Andromeda for analysis) to identify proteins that bind to or are post-translationally modified by phenanthriplatin or zinc in relevant cell lines (e.g., A549 lung cancer). Parallel untargeted metabolomics (using a Q-TOF platform and XCMS for feature detection) will map metal-induced changes in small molecule profiles. Isotopic tracing with 68Zn and enriched Pt isotopes will be used to distinguish specific metal-biomolecule complexes from background. Data integration will be performed using pathway analysis tools like MetaboAnalyst and STRING.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive 'fate map' for phenanthriplatin, identifying novel protein targets beyond DNA that may explain its unique cytotoxicity profile and potential synthetic lethal interactions. For zinc, it will define the specific proteomic and metabolomic networks engaged by its catalytic and signaling functions, directly linking to prior work on zinc-modulated sensory perception and beta-cell chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While metallodrug-protein interactions are often studied in isolation, the simultaneous application of advanced proteomic and metabolomic MS to both a next-generation synthetic platinum agent and native zinc biology is novel. This comparative approach will reveal fundamental principles of metal-biomolecule recognition across synthetic and native systems.

proposals.title: DNA-Encoded Activation of Inert Platinum(IV) Prodrugs via Selective Zinc-Catalyzed Reduction
proposals.motivation: Platinum(IV) prodrugs offer reduced toxicity compared to cisplatin but require intracellular reduction to the active Pt(II) species, a process often inefficient and non-selective. Concurrently, mobile zinc ions are known to catalyze specific redox reactions in biological environments, as demonstrated in β-cells. This project aims to merge these concepts by designing DNA sequences that not only bind specific inert Pt(IV) complexes but also harness endogenous zinc to trigger their selective activation, creating a new paradigm for targeted chemotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that G-quadruplex or specific hairpin DNA structures can be engineered to selectively bind inert Pt(IV) prodrugs and position them for efficient, zinc-catalyzed reduction to active Pt(II) species, thereby conferring both tumor-targeting and controlled activation.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational inverse design strategy using Rosetta DNA and NUPACK to generate DNA libraries predicted to bind model Pt(IV) complexes like tetraplatin. High-throughput selection (SELEX) will be performed against Pt(IV)-modified beads to isolate functional sequences. Hits will be characterized for binding affinity (ITC), structure (CD, NMR), and zinc-dependent reduction kinetics (UV-Vis, HPLC). Efficacy and zinc-dependent uptake will be tested in cancer cell lines with modulated zinc levels, comparing cytotoxicity and platinum-DNA adduct formation to phenanthriplatin as a control.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for using engineered DNA as a selective activator for inert metal-based prodrugs. It will provide a novel synthetic lethality strategy by co-targeting nucleotide excision repair (with platinum) and zinc homeostasis pathways. The project will deliver new design principles for bio-orthogonal catalysis in medicinal inorganic chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While DNA aptamers are known to bind small molecules, the intentional design of DNA to position a metal cofactor (Zn²⁺) for catalyzing the activation of a therapeutic metal complex (Pt(IV)) is unprecedented. This inverts the typical problem of designing metal complexes for DNA binding.

proposals.title: A Zinc-Phenanthriplatin Conjugate for Simultaneous Chemotherapy and Fluorescent Zinc Pool Imaging in Aneuploid Cancers
proposals.motivation: The clinical success of cisplatin is limited by resistance and severe side effects. Pre-2026 work by the Lippard group and others shows that phenanthriplatin, a monofunctional platinum(II) complex, has a unique mechanism of action and that mobile zinc pools modulate cellular stress responses and sensory pathways. Furthermore, synthetic lethality strategies targeting DNA repair and cell-cycle checkpoints (like those in aneuploid cells) are promising for enhancing chemotherapy. There is a critical need for agents that can both effectively kill cancer cells and report on their biochemical state in real time to guide treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single molecular conjugate combining a cytotoxic phenanthriplatin warhead with a zinc-responsive fluorescent sensor will exhibit augmented synthetic lethality in aneuploid cancer cells while enabling simultaneous imaging of therapy-induced fluctuations in labile zinc, which serve as a biomarker of cellular stress and treatment response.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of complexes where a phenanthriplatin analog is tethered to a turn-on fluorescent zinc sensor based on the ZinPyr or TSQ scaffold. Uptake and intracellular distribution will be quantified in isogenic cell lines with varying degrees of aneuploidy using flow cytometry and confocal microscopy. Cytotoxicity will be assessed via MTT assays, and synergy with ATR/CHK1 checkpoint inhibitors (synthetic lethality) will be evaluated. The zinc-specific fluorescence signal will be correlated with cell-cycle arrest (via flow cytometry) and DNA damage response (via γ-H2AX staining) to link zinc mobilization to therapeutic outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first theranostic agent that directly couples a next-generation platinum drug with a native metal ion imaging capability. It will establish a novel platform to study the role of mobile zinc in the mechanism of platinum-based chemotherapy and provide a tool for real-time, image-based assessment of drug efficacy and synthetic lethal interactions in live cells.
proposals.novelty: While zinc sensors and platinum drugs are well-studied separately, their covalent integration into a single, multifunctional complex designed to exploit synthetic lethality in aneuploidy is unprecedented. The design intentionally uses the therapeutic agent to perturb the very zinc pools it is engineered to image, creating a self-reporting chemotherapeutic.

proposals.title: A Dual-Targeting Zinc-Phenanthriplatin Conjugate for Augmented Synthetic Lethality in Aneuploid Cancers
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin resistance remains a major clinical challenge, often driven by enhanced DNA repair and altered cell-cycle checkpoints. Pre-2026 work from the Lippard lab and others has shown that co-targeting nucleotide excision repair (NER) and cell-cycle checkpoints can create potent synthetic lethality. Simultaneously, the lab's expertise in mobile zinc biology and the development of monofunctional platinum agents like phenanthriplatin provides a unique chemical platform to design multi-targeted systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a synthetic conjugate combining a DNA-damaging phenanthriplatin analog with a zinc-chelating/transporting moiety will achieve synergistic cytotoxicity by simultaneously inducing irreparable platinum-DNA lesions and dysregulating zinc-dependent proteins involved in DNA damage response and cell-cycle progression, particularly in aneuploid cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of conjugates linking a phenanthriplatin-derived scaffold to zinc-binding motifs (e.g., TPEN derivatives or Zinquin analogs) via hydrolyzable or self-immolative linkers. Uptake and intracellular zinc mobilization will be quantified in cancer cell lines using fluorescent zinc probes (e.g., FluoZin-3) and ICP-MS. The compounds' dual mechanism will be validated by assessing platinum-DNA adduct formation (via atomic absorption spectroscopy and antibody-based assays) and concomitant inhibition of zinc-finger NER proteins (e.g., XPA). Synergistic cell kill and synthetic lethality will be evaluated in isogenic cell pairs with differing ploidy and DNA repair capacity, using viability assays, flow cytometry for cell-cycle analysis, and γ-H2AX staining for DNA damage.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of multi-targeted metallodrugs that exploit the biological role of zinc to enhance platinum-based chemotherapy. It will provide a mechanistic framework for applying synthetic lethality through simultaneous disruption of DNA integrity and metal-ion homeostasis, potentially offering a strategy to overcome cisplatin resistance. The project will also advance the fundamental understanding of how mobile zinc modulates cellular response to genotoxic stress.
proposals.novelty: While zinc chelators and platinum drugs have been studied separately, their covalent integration into a single, designed chemical entity that leverages the Lippard lab's specific expertise in both areas is novel. The approach uniquely combines the cell biology of mobile zinc with the targeted synthetic lethality strategy recently advanced by the lab, moving beyond simple combination therapy to a unified pharmacophore.

proposals.title: Tumor-Microenvironment-Triggered Release of Phenanthriplatin from Pt(IV) Prodrugs via Zinc-Catalyzed Reduction
proposals.motivation: Platinum(IV) prodrugs offer a strategy to mitigate the systemic toxicity of Pt(II) chemotherapeutics like cisplatin and phenanthriplatin, but their activation often relies on non-specific intracellular reduction. The tumor microenvironment (TME) features unique biochemical stimuli, such as elevated labile zinc, which is implicated in cancer progression and therapy resistance. Exploiting this specific TME component for selective drug activation could significantly improve therapeutic index and overcome limitations of current Pt(IV) designs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Pt(IV) prodrugs of phenanthriplatin, functionalized with specific coordinating ligands, will undergo selective, traceless reduction and active drug release catalyzed by elevated labile zinc ions in the tumor microenvironment, leading to enhanced cancer cell uptake and synthetic lethality in aneuploidy-prone cells.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Pt(IV) prodrugs based on the phenanthriplatin scaffold, incorporating axial ligands designed for zinc interaction (e.g., thioethers, carboxylates). Reduction kinetics and zinc-catalyzed release of the active Pt(II) species will be quantified using HPLC and ICP-MS. Cellular uptake and cytotoxicity will be evaluated in cancer cell lines with varying zinc status and aneuploidy, using co-treatment strategies to probe synthetic lethality with DNA repair inhibitors, as informed by recent work on nucleotide excision repair co-targeting.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, bioinspired trigger for Pt(IV) prodrug activation, directly linking TME zinc biology to chemotherapy delivery. It will provide a new chemical strategy to augment the potency of non-cisplatin agents like phenanthriplatin and create a framework for achieving tumor-selective cytotoxicity through metal-catalyzed release.
proposals.novelty: While zinc-sensitive probes are known, applying native zinc as a specific catalyst for the traceless release of a Pt(II) warhead from a Pt(IV) prodrug is a novel approach, distinct from conventional glutathione- or ascorbate-driven reduction mechanisms.

proposals.title: Folic Acid-Conjugated Cyclodextrin Hosts for Targeted Delivery of Phenanthriplatin and Zinc Chelators in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of platinum anticancer drugs like cisplatin and its analogs, including phenanthriplatin, is limited by systemic toxicity and poor tumor selectivity. Supramolecular delivery systems based on cyclodextrins offer a promising strategy to improve drug solubility and stability, but they lack inherent targeting. Concurrently, dysregulated zinc homeostasis is a recognized feature in many cancers, including aggressive triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), and can modulate chemoresistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conjugating the folic acid receptor-targeting ligand to a β-cyclodextrin host will create a supramolecular vehicle that selectively delivers a cytotoxic payload—a phenanthriplatin prodrug and a zinc-selective chelator—to folate receptor-α (FRα)-overexpressing TNBC cells, enhancing synthetic lethality by co-targeting DNA damage and zinc-mediated survival pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a β-cyclodextrin derivative conjugated to folic acid via a polyethylene glycol (PEG) spacer. This host will be used to form inclusion complexes with two guests: 1) a phenanthriplatin prodrug modified for host encapsulation, and 2) a membrane-permeable, zinc-selective fluorescent sensor (e.g., Zinpyr-1) that also acts as a chelator. Uptake and selectivity will be quantified in FRα+ vs. FRα- cell lines using flow cytometry and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) for platinum. Efficacy and synthetic lethal interactions will be assessed via cell viability assays, cell-cycle analysis, and measurements of aneuploidy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, dual-action supramolecular platform that combines tumor-targeted platinum delivery with zinc modulation. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay between intracellular zinc pools and platinum-induced DNA damage response, potentially identifying a new co-targeting strategy to overcome chemoresistance in hard-to-treat cancers.
proposals.novelty: While targeted cyclodextrins exist, their application to co-deliver a monofunctional platinum agent (phenanthriplatin) and a zinc chelator is unexplored. This approach uniquely integrates Lippard lab expertise in platinum chemistry, mobile zinc biology, and synthetic lethality into a single, tumor-selective vehicle.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Microtiter Assay for the Cytotoxic Profiling of Pt(IV) Prodrug Libraries Using Zinc-Responsive Fluorescent Reporters
proposals.motivation: Pt(IV) prodrugs offer a promising strategy to improve the selectivity and reduce the side effects of platinum-based chemotherapeutics like cisplatin. However, their rational design is hindered by the lack of efficient, cell-based screening platforms to rapidly evaluate how structural modifications in the axial ligands affect cellular uptake, reduction, and cytotoxicity. Pre-2026 work from the Lippard lab has established the critical role of mobile zinc in modulating cellular responses to stress and the utility of synthetic lethality concepts for enhancing platinum efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a miniaturized, high-throughput assay using zinc-responsive fluorescent probes (e.g., Zinpyr-1) as reporters of cellular stress and death will enable the rapid and reliable ranking of novel Pt(IV) complex libraries based on their cytotoxic potency and mechanism of action.
proposals.method: We will adapt a 384-well microtiter plate format using cisplatin-sensitive and -resistant cancer cell lines. Cells will be treated with libraries of Pt(IV) complexes featuring diverse axial ligands. Cytotoxicity will be quantified in parallel using a standard resazurin (Alamar Blue) metabolic assay and a ratiometric zinc-responsive fluorescent dye (Zinpyr-1), capitalizing on the known dysregulation of mobile zinc during apoptosis. High-content imaging will be employed to measure single-cell fluorescence intensity and morphological changes (e.g., nuclear condensation) for a subset of hits to validate the mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, rapid screening platform for the next generation of Pt(IV) prodrugs, directly linking chemical structure to biological activity. It will provide new insights into how axial ligand chemistry influences prodrug activation and whether potent compounds induce zinc-specific stress pathways, potentially revealing new synthetic lethality targets.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput screening of platinum compounds exists, integrating a zinc-specific fluorescent readout with cytotoxicity measures is novel, leveraging the lab's expertise in zinc biology to create a more mechanistically informative primary screen for Pt(IV) complexes.

proposals.title: A Supramolecular Zinc-Phenanthriplatin Theranostic Agent for Real-Time Imaging and Augmented Synthetic Lethality in Cancer Cells
proposals.motivation: The clinical success of cisplatin is limited by severe side effects and intrinsic or acquired resistance. Pre-2026 work from the Lippard lab has shown that co-targeting nucleotide excision repair and cell-cycle checkpoints can augment synthetic lethality, and that zinc can catalyze selective molecular release in cells. A theranostic platform that combines real-time drug tracking with enhanced mechanistic action could address these limitations and personalize chemotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: A supramolecular complex, integrating a zinc-based imaging sensor for labile Zn²⁺ with the monofunctional platinum agent phenanthriplatin, will enable simultaneous fluorescence imaging of drug localization and delivery, while the zinc component potentiates phenanthriplatin's cytotoxicity through disruption of zinc homeostasis and synthetic lethal interactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a modular complex featuring a high-affinity, turn-on fluorescent zinc sensor (e.g., Zinpyr-family derivative) covalently linked, via a traceless linker, to phenanthriplatin. The construct will be designed for cellular uptake and intracellular release of both agents, triggered by the native zinc-catalyzed chemistry previously established in the lab. We will quantify uptake and intracellular trafficking in cancer cell lines using fluorescence microscopy and ICP-MS, and correlate imaging data with cytotoxicity assays and markers of aneuploidy and DNA damage response.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first theranostic agent that directly links the subcellular distribution of a Pt drug to its mechanism of action, providing a tool to study synthetic lethality in real time. It will establish a novel strategy to enhance platinum chemotherapy by co-opting cellular zinc biology, moving beyond passive delivery to active potentiation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the intentional fusion of zinc-based catalytic release and sensing with a non-cisplatin-like platinum warhead (phenanthriplatin) to create a single chemical entity that is both an imaging probe and a mechanism-based combination therapy, a concept not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Clonal and Spatial Mapping of Zinc Dysregulation and DNA Repair Co-evolution in Cisplatin-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin resistance remains a major clinical challenge in solid tumors like ovarian cancer. Pre-2026 work has identified multiple potential resistance mechanisms, including enhanced nucleotide excision repair (NER), altered zinc homeostasis (which affects cisplatin uptake and metallothionein binding), and cell-cycle checkpoint adaptations. However, it is unknown whether these pathways evolve in a mutually exclusive or co-operative manner within the complex architecture of a single tumor, limiting rational combinatorial therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within a single tumor, distinct subclones spatially segregate primary resistance mechanisms (e.g., NER upregulation vs. zinc transporter dysregulation), but that upon therapeutic pressure, surviving subclones co-opt multiple, complementary pathways, leading to polyclonal, multifactorial resistance.
proposals.method: We will perform multi-region sampling from patient-matched treatment-naïve and post-cisplatin-relapse high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma specimens. For each region, we will use single-cell RNA-seq (10x Genomics) to define subclones and profile expression of resistance pathways (e.g., ERCC1, CTR1, ZnT transporters, metallothioneins). We will isolate subpopulations via FACS based on zinc flux (using Fluozin-3 AM) and DNA repair capacity (using a γH2AX flow cytometry assay). These isolated cells will be subjected to parallel high-throughput drug screens with cisplatin, phenanthriplatin, and NER/targeted synthetically lethal agents (e.g., ATR inhibitors). Data integration will use Bayesian clustering and phylogenetic inference to map mechanism co-occurrence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatially and clonally resolved map of how fundamental biochemical resistance mechanisms—metal ion homeostasis and DNA repair—co-evolve. It will directly inform whether co-targeting these pathways is a viable strategy against a single resistant clone or if it requires targeting multiple clones simultaneously. The functional validation bridges genomic observations with actionable therapeutic profiles.
proposals.novelty: While intratumor heterogeneity is recognized, linking specific, functionally validated biochemical resistance mechanisms (zinc biology and DNA repair) to subclonal architecture and spatial niches in a paired pre-/post-treatment setting is novel. The approach moves beyond cataloging mutations to defining operable, co-occurring phenotypic pathways within single cells.

proposals.title: Synthetic Lethality Induced by Hyperactive Nucleotide Excision Repair: Exploiting Genomic Rigidity for Cancer Therapy
proposals.motivation: Resistance to platinum-based chemotherapeutics like cisplatin is frequently linked to enhanced DNA repair capacity, particularly in nucleotide excision repair (NER). However, the paradoxical genomic consequences of hyperactive DNA repair remain underexplored. This project seeks to challenge the monolithic view of repair upregulation as purely protective by investigating whether it creates unique, targetable vulnerabilities through synthetic lethality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineered hyperactivation of specific DNA repair pathways (e.g., NER via ERCC1 overexpression or base excision repair via POLB) will induce a state of 'genomic rigidity' that is synthetically lethal with secondary agents that disrupt replication or induce specific DNA lesions.
proposals.method: We will establish isogenic cancer cell lines with doxycycline-inducible overexpression of ERCC1 or POLB, validated by immunoblotting and functional repair assays. These lines and controls will be screened against a focused library of secondary agents, including ATR inhibitors (VE-822), nucleoside analogs (gemcitabine), and ROS-inducers (phenanthriplatin), to identify synthetic lethal interactions via high-throughput viability assays. Hits will be mechanistically characterized by measuring replication stress (γH2AX, RPA foci), mitotic defects, and chromosomal instability (aneuploidy) via flow cytometry and imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a new therapeutic paradigm that reinterprets a major resistance mechanism as a source of vulnerability. It will provide a functional map of synthetic lethal interactions specific to hyperactive repair states, potentially identifying novel combination strategies for cisplatin-resistant cancers.
proposals.novelty: The concept directly inverts the standard resistance narrative by targeting over-reinforced repair, rather than its inhibition. It integrates Lippard's expertise in metal-based agents and cellular response with modern synthetic lethality screening, moving beyond BRCA paradigms to novel, repair-induced fragility.

proposals.title: Real-Time Imaging of Zinc Dysregulation as a Consequence of Hyperactive DNA Repair and a Driver of Genomic Instability
proposals.motivation: Hyperactive DNA repair, while counterintuitive, can promote genomic instability and sensitize cells to chemotherapeutics like cisplatin, a phenomenon linked to synthetic lethality. Concurrently, mobile zinc (Zn2+) is a critical signaling ion, and its dysregulation is implicated in stress responses and cell death. No study has directly visualized the dynamic zinc fluxes that may mechanistically connect aberrant DNA repair activity to downstream genomic chaos and therapeutic vulnerability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hyperactive DNA repair disrupts intracellular zinc homeostasis, creating spatiotemporally defined Zn2+ fluxes that directly contribute to increased genomic instability and modulate cellular sensitivity to genotoxic agents.
proposals.method: We will engineer a rationetric FRET-based Zn2+ sensor by integrating a Zinpyr-1-like chelator (high Zn2+ affinity, selectivity over Ca2+) with cyan and yellow fluorescent proteins. This construct will be targeted to the nucleus and cytoplasm of isogenic cell lines with hyperactive nucleotide excision repair (NER), generated via overexpression of key NER factors (e.g., ERCC1-XPF). Using live-cell fluorescence microscopy, we will correlate real-time Zn2+ dynamics with markers of DNA damage (γ-H2AX foci) and cell fate in response to cisplatin and phenanthriplatin, agents with distinct repair vulnerabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, mechanistic link between DNA repair status and zinc ion biology, providing a novel metabolic explanation for repair-induced instability. It will deliver a powerful subcellular imaging tool for the metals in cancer biology community and identify zinc dysregulation as a potential new target for augmenting synthetic lethal chemotherapy strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, real-time visualization of Zn2+ as a secondary messenger of DNA repair hyperactivity, a concept not previously explored, and the fusion of rationetric metal sensing with models of pathological DNA repair to explain genomic instability.

proposals.title: Dynamic Feedback-Controlled Chemotherapy: Real-Time Imaging of Redox-Active Metal Flux to Guide Detoxification Inhibition and Enhance Platinum Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Cellular detoxification pathways, often involving metallothioneins and glutathione-dependent enzymes that utilize redox-active metals like Cu(I) and Fe(II), are a major mechanism of resistance to platinum-based chemotherapeutics like cisplatin. Current strategies to inhibit these pathways are static and do not account for the dynamic, heterogeneous metabolic response of tumors, potentially limiting their efficacy and increasing off-target toxicity. A method to directly visualize the activation of these metal-dependent detoxification systems in real time would enable a more precise therapeutic intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intracellular flux of redox-active metal cofactors (Cu(I)/Fe(II)) is a direct, real-time reporter of detoxification pathway activation following platinum exposure, and that sensor-guided, temporally optimized administration of detoxification inhibitors will significantly increase platinum-DNA adduct formation and cell death compared to co-administration.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize turn-on fluorescent sensors for Cu(I) and Fe(II) based on a fluorescein-photoelectron transfer (PET) scaffold, building on principles from Lippard's Zn²⁺ sensor work. Sensor specificity and dynamic range will be validated in vitro. Using live-cell fluorescence microscopy, we will monitor real-time metal flux in non-small cell lung cancer cells treated with cisplatin or phenanthriplatin. We will correlate sensor signal with detoxification protein expression (e.g., metallothionein) and platinum uptake/DNA adduct formation. Finally, we will use the sensor signal as a trigger to administer candidate inhibitors (e.g., glutathione synthesis inhibitors) and measure the resultant enhancement in platinum-induced synthetic lethality and aneuploidy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first tools to visualize the dynamic metabolic response of metal-based detoxification to platinum chemotherapy. It will establish a direct link between real-time metal cofactor flux, detoxification activity, and platinum efficacy. The proposed sensor-guided combination strategy provides a novel paradigm for personalizing and timing adjuvant therapy to overcome resistance.
proposals.novelty: While metal sensors exist, their application as dynamic reporters to guide the timing of adjuvant therapy in cancer is novel. This moves beyond static co-treatment to a feedback-controlled system, directly targeting the metabolic activity that drives resistance rather than just the proteins involved.

proposals.title: A Synthetic Toxin-Antitoxin System for Selective Apoptosis in Cisplatin-Treated Cancer Cells via Zinc-Mediated Antitoxin Degradation
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin resistance remains a major clinical challenge, often due to impaired apoptosis signaling despite successful DNA damage induction. Pre-2026 work shows that mobile zinc (Zn²⁺) fluxes are dysregulated in cancer and can be modulated, and that synthetic lethality strategies co-targeting DNA repair and cell-cycle checkpoints can enhance chemotherapy. This proposal seeks to create a more precise, conditional lethality by exploiting the unique biochemical environment of cisplatin-stressed cells as a trigger.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a labile, zinc-chelating 'antitoxin' molecule can be co-administered with cisplatin and a potent apoptotic 'toxin' (e.g., a direct caspase activator). The elevated labile Zn²⁺ pool in cisplatin-damaged cancer cells will selectively degrade the antitoxin via chelation and displacement, unleashing the toxin to trigger apoptosis specifically in the targeted cells.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of photocleavable or hydrolytically labile zinc chelators (e.g., based on TPEN derivatives) as candidate antitoxins, whose Zn²⁺ binding affinity and stability are tuned. Phenanthriplatin will be used as a model apoptotic toxin due to its distinct uptake and mechanism. Using pre-2026 methods, we will test the system in cancer cell lines, measuring antitoxin degradation via zinc fluorophore competition (e.g., FluoZin-3), intracellular toxin activation via a fluorescent caspase-3 reporter, and cell death synergy with cisplatin using viability and aneuploidy assays. Selectivity will be assessed in non-malignant cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, bioinspired 'synthetic toxin-antitoxin' paradigm for cancer therapy that spatially and temporally couples the stress signal (Pt-DNA damage) to the death effector. It will provide a new chemical strategy to lower the apoptotic threshold specifically in therapy-resistant cells, moving beyond traditional synthetic lethality by incorporating a direct, conditional activation switch.
proposals.novelty: While zinc modulation and synthetic lethality are known, the conceptual adaptation of bacterial TA system logic to mammalian cell death signaling via a synthetic, zinc-sensitive chemical circuit is novel. This approach creates a AND-gate (cisplatin stress + specific microenvironment) for toxin activation, offering a new layer of specificity.

proposals.title: Mapping Microenvironment-Driven Adaptive Resistance to Platinum Drugs via Longitudinal CRISPR Screens in Patient-Derived Tumor Organoids
proposals.motivation: Platinum resistance remains a major clinical obstacle, and conventional screens in monocultures fail to capture the critical role of the tumor microenvironment (TME) in promoting survival. Pre-2026 work, including from the Lippard lab, highlights how cellular zinc and calcium signaling can modulate drug uptake and DNA damage response, processes heavily influenced by stromal and immune cell crosstalk. A systematic, functional map of resistance pathways that emerge specifically within a dynamic, heterogeneous TME during cyclic chemotherapy is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that repeated platinum exposure within a physiologically relevant TME selects for unique, adaptive resistance pathways driven by tumor-stroma interactions, which are distinct from those identified in static monolayer cultures.
proposals.method: We will establish patient-derived non-small cell lung cancer organoids co-cultured with cancer-associated fibroblasts and macrophages. These complex cultures will be transduced with a genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout library and subjected to longitudinal, pulsed treatments with cisplatin or phenanthriplatin, mimicking clinical cycles. Deep sequencing of sgRNA representation pre- and post-treatment will identify genes whose loss confers a selective survival advantage specifically under TME pressure. Hits will be validated using orthogonal assays measuring drug uptake, DNA damage (γ-H2AX), and aneuploidy.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first functional atlas of TME-dependent platinum resistance genes, revealing novel synthetic lethal targets whose inhibition could overcome microenvironment-mediated chemoprotection. It will establish a transformative screening platform for studying adaptive therapy resistance in a clinically relevant context.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the longitudinal application of genome-wide CRISPR screening within a dynamic, multi-cellular TME model under cyclic drug pressure, directly testing how non-tumor cell interactions rewire essential survival pathways during treatment.

proposals.title: Synthetic Lethality Targeting of DNA Polymerase Eta in Nucleotide Excision Repair-Proficient, Platinum-Resistant Cancers
proposals.motivation: Platinum resistance in cancers like high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC) remains a major clinical challenge. While synthetic lethality of PARP inhibitors in BRCA-deficient cancers is established, many resistant tumors are proficient in nucleotide excision repair (NER), the primary pathway for removing platinum adducts. New strategies are needed to induce vulnerability in these NER-proficient, resistant cancers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of NER-proficient, platinum-resistant cancers have acquired deficiencies in translesion synthesis (TLS) polymerases, creating a synthetic lethal vulnerability when co-targeted with ATR or CHK1 inhibitors, analogous to the BRCA-PARP paradigm.
proposals.method: We will establish a panel of isogenic, phenanthriplatin-resistant cell lines from NER-proficient models (e.g., A2780) and profile their DNA repair capacity using a combination of comet assays, immunofluorescence for γH2AX/53BP1 foci, and a CRISPR-Cas9 screen targeting DNA damage response genes. Synthetic lethal interactions will be identified by treating resistant lines with a library of targeted agents (ATRi, CHK1i, WEE1i) and measuring cell viability and aneuploidy via flow cytometry. Validation will involve siRNA knockdown of candidate TLS polymerases (e.g., Pol η, Pol ζ) and assessing potentiation of phenanthriplatin or cisplatin cytotoxicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify a novel synthetic lethal pair for NER-proficient, platinum-resistant cancers, expanding the therapeutic landscape beyond BRCA mutations. It will provide a mechanistic rationale for combining specific targeted agents with platinum-based chemotherapy or next-generation agents like phenanthriplatin to overcome resistance.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond targeting canonical homologous recombination and applies the synthetic lethality framework to vulnerabilities created by acquired resistance itself, specifically probing the interplay between NER and TLS in a clinically relevant, resistant context.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Profiling of Platinum-DNA Adducts and DNA Damage Response Proteins Reveals Determinants of Cisplatin Resistance Heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin efficacy is limited by intrinsic and acquired resistance, often attributed to variable drug uptake, DNA repair capacity, and cell-cycle checkpoint proficiency at the population level. However, bulk analyses mask critical single-cell heterogeneity in initial platinum-DNA adduct formation and the subsequent cellular response, obscuring the key determinants of treatment failure. This gap hinders the rational design of synthetic lethal combinations, such as those targeting nucleotide excision repair (NER) and cell-cycle checkpoints, to overcome resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-cell heterogeneity in cisplatin response is driven by correlated variability in initial platinum-DNA adduct levels and the concurrent expression of key proteins involved in NER (e.g., ERCC1) and cell-cycle checkpoints (e.g., p53, phosphorylated CHK1).
proposals.method: We will employ mass cytometry (CyTOF) for multiplexed single-cell analysis. Cells (e.g., A549 lung cancer) treated with cisplatin or phenanthriplatin will be stained with a cisplatin- or platinum-specific antibody to quantify DNA adducts, alongside metal-tagged antibodies for target proteins (ERCC1, p53, pCHK1). A barcoding approach will be used to minimize technical variance. Data analysis will involve viSNE or t-SNE for dimensionality reduction and visualization, followed by PhenoGraph clustering to identify distinct cellular subpopulations based on adduct load and protein expression profiles, correlating these states with cell fate via a viability marker.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative, correlative map between platinum-DNA damage and the protein-level DNA damage response in single cells. It will identify specific resistant cellular phenotypes that could be targeted by augmented synthetic lethality strategies. Furthermore, it will provide a novel analytical platform applicable to other metallodrugs, linking the Lippard group's expertise in mobile zinc biology and platinum anticancer agents.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell proteomics and platinum adduct detection exist separately, their simultaneous measurement to directly link causative lesion (Pt-DNA adduct) with mechanistic cellular response (protein expression) in the same cell is novel. This approach moves beyond inferring relationships from bulk studies to directly testing them at the resolution where therapeutic decisions are made.

proposals.title: Pan-Cancer Analysis of Genomic and Transcriptomic Signatures Reveals Conserved and Divergent Pathways in Platinum Drug Resistance
proposals.motivation: Platinum-based chemotherapeutics like cisplatin remain cornerstone treatments for numerous cancers, but intrinsic and acquired resistance severely limits their efficacy. While resistance mechanisms have been studied in specific cancer types, a systematic, pan-cancer analysis is lacking, hindering the development of universal strategies to overcome resistance. Aggregating multi-omics data across cancer types can distinguish tumor-specific adaptations from core, conserved resistance pathways, which is crucial for designing rational combination therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pan-cancer meta-analysis will reveal a limited set of conserved genomic and transcriptomic alterations—involving DNA damage response, metal ion homeostasis, and cell cycle regulation—that underpin a core platinum resistance phenotype, alongside unique, tissue-specific resistance signatures.
proposals.method: We will aggregate and uniformly process publicly available pre-2026 genomic (mutations, copy number variations) and transcriptomic data from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) and other repositories for cisplatin-treated samples across ovarian, lung, testicular, and head and neck cancers. Using established bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., GATK for variant calling, DESeq2 for differential expression), we will identify resistance-associated features. We will then apply non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) and pathway enrichment analysis (using MSigDB) to deconvolute pan-cancer versus cancer-type-specific resistance modules. Validation will involve correlating signatures with in vitro drug response data (e.g., from GDSC) and testing synthetic lethal partners using siRNA screens in isogenic resistant cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, data-driven map of common and unique platinum resistance pathways across major cancer types. It will nominate high-priority, conserved therapeutic targets for combination with platinum drugs and identify biomarkers for patient stratification. The findings will directly inform the design of augmented synthetic lethality strategies, a core theme in the Lippard lab's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies focus on single cancer types, the novelty lies in the systematic, cross-tissue integration of multi-omics data to disentangle universal from context-dependent resistance mechanisms. This approach directly connects the lab's expertise in cisplatin action and metal biology (e.g., zinc modulation) to large-scale clinical genomics, offering a new framework to understand treatment failure.

proposals.title: Small-Molecule PSMA-Targeted Zinc Probes for Enhanced Cisplatin Uptake and Synthetic Lethality in Prostate Cancer
proposals.motivation: Prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) is a validated target for prostate cancer imaging and therapy, but current aptamer-based targeting ligands suffer from complex synthesis, immunogenicity, and poor tissue penetration. Pre-2026 work from the Lippard lab has established mobile zinc as a key modulator of cellular signaling and demonstrated that synthetic zinc complexes can be engineered for selective release and biological activity. This project aims to merge these themes to develop more efficient, small-molecule drug delivery systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing nucleic acid aptamers with small-molecule PSMA inhibitors (e.g., glutamate-urea-based ligands) to deliver synthetic zinc-chelator complexes will enhance cellular uptake and selectively disrupt zinc homeostasis in prostate cancer cells, thereby augmenting the cytotoxicity of co-administered platinum drugs like cisplatin through a synthetic lethality mechanism.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of conjugates linking established small-molecule PSMA inhibitors (e.g., DUPA-PEG) to zinc-responsive fluorophores (e.g., Zinpyr-family probes) and to synthetic zinc ionophores. Uptake and specificity will be quantified in PSMA+ vs. PSMA- cell lines using flow cytometry and confocal microscopy. The most effective conjugates will be co-administered with cisplatin or phenanthriplatin, and cell viability, platinum-DNA adduct formation, and induction of aneuploidy will be measured. Synthetic lethality will be assessed by co-targeting nucleotide excision repair (inhibited by platinum) and zinc-disrupted cell-cycle checkpoints.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of targeted, zinc-delivering small molecules for cancer therapy. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how targeted zinc dysregulation can be leveraged as an adjuvant to platinum chemotherapy, specifically in prostate cancer. The project will yield simplified, more pharmacologically viable targeting strategies compared to aptamer-based systems.
proposals.novelty: While PSMA-targeted small molecules and zinc probes exist separately, their integration into a single therapeutic agent designed to exploit zinc biology for enhancing platinum drug response is novel. The approach uniquely applies the lab's expertise in synthetic zinc biology and cisplatin mechanisms to the problem of targeted drug delivery.

proposals.title: Zinc-Finger Nanoparticles for Tumor-Microenvironment-Triggered Release of Phenanthriplatin and Aneuploidy-Inducing Agents
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of platinum-based chemotherapeutics like cisplatin and phenanthriplatin is limited by systemic toxicity and resistance mechanisms, including enhanced nucleotide excision repair (NER). The tumor microenvironment (TME) offers unique biochemical cues, such as lowered pH and elevated protease activity, which can be exploited for targeted drug release. Integrating these stimuli-responsive strategies with Lippard's expertise in mobile zinc biology and synthetic lethality could create more selective and potent anticancer agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a zinc-based nanoparticle platform, incorporating pH-sensitive (e.g., hydrazone) and/or enzyme-cleavable (e.g., matrix metalloproteinase-9 substrate) linkers to tether phenanthriplatin and an NER-targeting agent, will selectively release its payload in the TME, leading to augmented synthetic lethality and enhanced tumor cell death.
proposals.method: We will synthesize zinc-based coordination polymer nanoparticles (CPNs) or zinc-doped silica nanoparticles. Phenanthriplatin and a selected NER inhibitor (e.g., a small molecule targeting ERCC1) will be conjugated to the nanoparticle matrix via a pH-sensitive hydrazone linker and an MMP-9-cleavable peptide linker, respectively. Nanoparticle uptake and intracellular zinc release will be quantified in cancer cell lines using established fluorescent probes (e.g., Zinpyr-1). Drug release kinetics will be measured in vitro under TME-mimicking conditions (pH 6.5, added MMP-9). The synthetic lethal effect of co-released payloads will be assessed via cell viability assays, comet assays for DNA damage, and flow cytometry to monitor cell-cycle arrest and aneuploidy induction.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, stimuli-responsive drug delivery platform that merges Lippard's foundational work in zinc biology and platinum anticancer agents. It will provide a direct chemical method to test the synthetic lethality hypothesis of co-targeting NER and cell-cycle checkpoints with spatiotemporal control in the TME. The project is expected to yield a new class of 'smart' chemotherapeutic agents with improved therapeutic indices.
proposals.novelty: While stimuli-responsive nanoparticles exist, the specific integration of zinc as a catalytic or structural component, combined with the co-delivery of phenanthriplatin and an NER-targeting agent via orthogonal release mechanisms, is unprecedented. This approach uniquely leverages Lippard's recent findings on native zinc catalysis and augmented synthetic lethality within a single, tumor-targeted platform.

proposals.title: A Zinc-Facilitated Co-Delivery System for Cisplatin and a Nucleotide Excision Repair Inhibitor to Overcome Chemoresistance
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin resistance remains a major clinical obstacle, often driven by enhanced DNA repair, particularly nucleotide excision repair (NER). Pre-2026 work from the Lippard lab has established synthetic lethality strategies targeting NER and cell-cycle checkpoints, and has pioneered the use of zinc ions for selective, traceless release of molecules in cells. Combining these insights to co-deliver cisplatin with a NER inhibitor directly into cancer cells could dramatically enhance therapeutic efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single-molecule, zinc-responsive prodrug conjugate, which co-releases cisplatin and a potent NER inhibitor (e.g., a small-molecule targeting ERCC1-XPF), will selectively kill cisplatin-resistant cancer cells by simultaneously inducing DNA damage and blocking its primary repair pathway.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a heterobifunctional prodrug by tethering a cisplatin analog (e.g., phenanthriplatin for enhanced cellular uptake) and a NER inhibitor via a zinc-cleavable linker, inspired by the lab's work on native zinc catalysis. The construct's uptake, zinc-triggered release kinetics, and intracellular fate will be quantified using ICP-MS and fluorescence assays. Efficacy will be tested in paired cisplatin-sensitive and -resistant cell lines, measuring cell viability, platinum-DNA adduct formation (via atomic absorption), NER activity (via host-cell reactivation assay), and induction of synthetic lethal aneuploidy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel chemical strategy for overcoming platinum resistance via targeted co-delivery, directly translating synthetic lethality concepts into a single-molecule therapeutic. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how simultaneous drug release within a cell amplifies DNA damage and cell death. The project will also expand the application of zinc-based release mechanisms from sensing to targeted combination chemotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While combination therapies exist, the innovation lies in the zinc-catalyzed, co-localized release of both agents from a single construct, ensuring they reach the same cell at the same time, and the direct integration of a NER inhibitor with a next-generation platinum agent like phenanthriplatin.

proposals.title: Spin-State-Dependent Oxygen Activation by Synthetic Fe(II) Complexes: A Mechanistic Bridge to Metallodrug Reactivity
proposals.motivation: The spin state of Fe(II) centers in metalloproteins critically controls O2 binding and activation, dictating biological outcomes from respiration to DNA repair. In synthetic bioinorganic chemistry, this fundamental relationship remains underexplored, particularly for complexes relevant to metallodrug mechanisms. Understanding how spin state governs O2 reactivity could reveal new principles for designing metal-based therapeutics that exploit cellular oxygen, connecting to the lab's expertise in cisplatin action and metal ion biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spin state (high-spin vs. low-spin) of synthetic, coordinatively unsaturated Fe(II) complexes will dictate the kinetic pathway and thermodynamic stability of O2 adducts, thereby controlling whether O2 activation proceeds via reversible binding, oxidative decomposition, or generation of reactive oxygen species.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Fe(II) complexes with pentadentate ligands of tunable field strength to enforce specific spin states, confirmed by SQUID magnetometry and Mössbauer spectroscopy. O2 reactivity will be monitored in real-time using stopped-flow UV-vis and low-temperature spectroscopy. Product analysis will employ ESI-MS and EPR to identify intermediates and final oxidation products. The synthetic lethality concept from our recent work will guide cellular assays using phenanthriplatin-treated cancer cell models to probe the biological consequences of spin-state-modulated O2 activation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish clear design rules linking Fe(II) electronic structure to O2 activation pathways in synthetic systems. It will provide a foundational mechanistic framework for developing new metallodrugs that leverage cellular O2 for selective toxicity, potentially augmenting chemotherapy response. The findings will also offer new insights into the role of metal ion spin in biological sensing and catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While spin-state effects are known in heme proteins, systematically controlling and probing this parameter in synthetic, non-heme Fe(II) models to direct O2 activation pathways is a novel synthetic and mechanistic challenge. This approach directly connects fundamental inorganic reactivity to the lab's core themes of metal-based cellular perturbation and therapeutic design.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of Non-DNA Protein Targets for Platinum and Zinc-Based Anticancer Agents Using Chemical Proteomics and Synthetic Lethality Screening
proposals.motivation: The paradigm that DNA is the primary target for platinum anticancer drugs like cisplatin is well-established, yet significant gaps remain in understanding the full spectrum of their cellular interactions and mechanisms of cytotoxicity. Recent work from the Lippard lab on mobile zinc signaling and the development of phenanthriplatin, which exhibits a distinct mechanism from cisplatin, suggests that metal complexes can engage potent non-genomic pathways. A systematic investigation into protein targets is crucial for understanding drug resistance, side effects, and for designing next-generation metallodrugs with novel mechanisms of action.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the cytotoxicity of clinically relevant platinum drugs and novel zinc-based complexes is mediated by high-affinity interactions with specific cellular proteins, disrupting critical signaling or metabolic pathways independent of direct DNA damage.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach: 1) Chemical proteomics using cisplatin and phenanthriplatin derivatives equipped with photo-crosslinking and affinity tags (e.g., alkyne handles for click chemistry) to capture, identify, and quantify protein binding partners in cancer cell lysates and live cells. 2) Integration with synthetic lethality screening, using siRNA libraries to identify genes whose knockdown synergizes with drug treatment under conditions where global nucleotide excision repair is co-targeted, pinpointing proteins in complementary survival pathways. 3) Validation of top hits through direct binding assays (ITC, fluorescence anisotropy) and functional studies measuring downstream effects on zinc homeostasis, calcium signaling, and cell-cycle progression in relevant cell models.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive, direct inventory of protein targets for key platinum and zinc-based anticancer agents, moving beyond the DNA-centric view. It will establish a new framework for understanding metallodrug mechanisms and provide validated protein targets for the rational design of novel metal-based therapeutics that exploit synthetic lethal interactions.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has inferred protein interactions indirectly, this proposal directly integrates active compound proteomic profiling with functional genetic screening (synthetic lethality) specifically in a metallodrug context, a combination not yet applied to this field. It leverages the lab's unique expertise in both zinc biology and platinum drug design to challenge a fundamental dogma.

proposals.title: Photoactivated Platinum(IV) Prodrugs for Spatiotemporal Control of DNA Damage and Synthetic Lethality in Cisplatin-Resistant Cancers
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin resistance remains a major clinical challenge, often driven by enhanced DNA repair, particularly nucleotide excision repair (NER). While Pt(IV) prodrugs offer improved stability and reduced side effects, their activation is typically reliant on intracellular reduction, which lacks spatial and temporal control. Light activation provides a powerful tool to generate cytotoxic lesions with precise timing and location, potentially overcoming resistance mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that novel, inert Pt(IV) prodrugs, designed with photo-labile axial ligands, will undergo light-induced reduction to release active Pt(II) species that create unique, less repairable DNA lesions, and that this spatiotemporal control can be leveraged to induce synthetic lethality when combined with cell-cycle checkpoint inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Pt(IV) complexes with axial ligands tuned for specific photoactivation wavelengths (e.g., visible light). Their photochemistry, stability, and cellular uptake will be characterized. Using established protocols from the Lippard lab, we will evaluate DNA binding profiles, lesion formation, and repair kinetics via atomic absorption spectroscopy and comet assays. The most promising prodrugs will be tested in combination with ATR or CHK1 inhibitors (synthetic lethality approach) in NER-proficient and -deficient cancer cell lines, assessing cell viability, aneuploidy, and cell-cycle arrest.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of photopharmacological tools for cancer research, providing a mechanism to study DNA damage response with high precision. It will demonstrate a novel strategy to bypass NER-mediated cisplatin resistance by generating irreparable lesions and exploiting synthetic lethal interactions, potentially leading to more effective and targeted chemotherapy regimens.
proposals.novelty: The integration of photoactivation with Pt(IV) prodrug design to create spatiotemporally controlled, less repairable DNA lesions is novel. Combining this precise activation with a co-targeting synthetic lethality strategy (NER + cell-cycle checkpoints) represents a significant advance beyond conventional prodrug development.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Zinc-Phenanthriplatin Host-Guest Complexes for Enhanced DNA Targeting and Cellular Uptake
proposals.motivation: The clinical success of cisplatin is limited by resistance and toxicity. While phenanthriplatin, a monofunctional platinum(II) complex, shows a distinct cytotoxic profile and mechanism, its cellular uptake and DNA-binding efficiency can be suboptimal. Supramolecular chemistry offers a powerful, pre-2026 strategy to engineer drug delivery and targeting by modulating solubility, membrane transport, and local concentration at biological targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that encapsulating phenanthriplatin within a zinc-cyclen-based supramolecular host will enhance its cellular uptake and facilitate a traceless release mechanism at the nucleus, leading to increased DNA adduct formation and cytotoxicity, particularly in cancer cells with defects in DNA repair pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of zinc-cyclen derivatives functionalized with hydrophobic or cationic groups to act as water-soluble hosts for phenanthriplatin, confirmed by NMR and ITC. Host-guest binding dynamics and zinc-catalyzed release will be studied spectroscopically. Cellular uptake of the complexes versus free drug will be quantified using ICP-MS for Pt and Zn. Cytotoxicity (IC50) and DNA damage response (γ-H2AX foci) will be assessed in paired cell lines with proficient and deficient nucleotide excision repair (NER), and synthetic lethality with ATR/CHK1 checkpoint inhibitors will be evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for designing metallodrugs using supramolecular principles, directly linking enhanced delivery to mechanism of action. It will provide a novel strategy to augment synthetic lethality by co-targeting NER and cell-cycle checkpoints with a supramolecular prodrug system. The findings could lead to next-generation platinum agents with improved therapeutic indices.
proposals.novelty: While zinc-cyclen units are known for zinc sensing and traceless release, their application as programmable, catalytic delivery vehicles for platinum anticancer agents is unexplored. This approach uniquely combines Lippard's expertise in mobile zinc biology, synthetic platinum chemistry, and DNA damage response to create a new class of 'supramolecular metallodrugs'.

proposals.title: Antibody-Directed Platinum-Phenanthriplatin Conjugates for Targeted DNA Damage in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin and its derivatives remain cornerstone chemotherapeutics but suffer from dose-limiting systemic toxicity and acquired resistance, partly due to inefficient cellular uptake and DNA adduct repair. The Lippard lab's development of phenanthriplatin, a monofunctional platinum(IV) prodrug with enhanced cellular accumulation and a distinct mechanism of action, offers a promising scaffold. However, its tumor selectivity is limited. Concurrently, antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) have revolutionized targeted cancer therapy, but their payloads are typically organic toxins, not DNA-targeting metal complexes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that site-specific bioconjugation of a reducible platinum(IV) prodrug derivative of phenanthriplatin to a tumor-specific monoclonal antibody (e.g., targeting Trop-2 or EGFR common in TNBC) will enable selective delivery, intracellular reduction to the active platinum(II) species, and potent, targeted DNA damage, thereby improving therapeutic index.
proposals.method: We will synthesize an asymmetric platinum(IV) prodrug analog of phenanthriplatin with one axial ligand functionalized for bioconjugation (e.g., a carboxylate or maleimide). This will be site-specifically conjugated to cysteine-engineered anti-Trop-2 antibodies via thiol-maleimide chemistry. Conjugate stability in serum and selective, receptor-mediated cellular uptake will be quantified in TNBC cell lines using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) for platinum and flow cytometry. DNA damage response (γ-H2AX foci) and synthetic lethal interactions (co-targeting nucleotide excision repair, as in recent lab work) will be assessed. Efficacy and systemic toxicity will be evaluated in vivo using patient-derived xenograft models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for antibody-targeted delivery of monofunctional platinum agents, directly merging the fields of bioinorganic chemistry and ADC technology. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how targeted delivery alters the cellular response to platinum-induced DNA damage, potentially uncovering new synthetic lethal vulnerabilities. The project aims to deliver a prototype therapeutic conjugate with enhanced tumor specificity and reduced off-target toxicity compared to conventional platinum chemotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While ADCs are established, their payloads have not included mechanistically distinct, synthetically tunable monofunctional platinum agents like phenanthriplatin. The approach is novel in leveraging the redox-activation chemistry of platinum(IV) prodrugs within the context of a targeted biologic, creating a unique 'metal-ADC' hybrid.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Platinum and Zinc Complexes to Generate DNA Lesions Resistant to Nucleotide Excision Repair
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of platinum anticancer drugs like cisplatin is limited by cellular DNA repair, particularly nucleotide excision repair (NER). While augmenting synthetic lethality by co-targeting NER is a promising strategy, an orthogonal approach is to design metal complexes that create intrinsically repair-resistant lesions. This would directly enhance cytotoxicity and overcome a primary resistance mechanism in cancer therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: By starting with known structural features of NER-resistant DNA lesions (e.g., bulky, non-distorting adducts), we can rationally design and synthesize novel platinum and zinc complexes that selectively form these adducts on DNA, leading to enhanced and persistent genomic damage in cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will first curate a database of DNA lesion structures with known NER susceptibility from literature. Using molecular docking and DFT calculations, we will model interactions between candidate metal complexes (e.g., phenanthriplatin analogs, zinc-salphen complexes) and DNA to predict adduct geometry. Promising candidates will be synthesized and their DNA binding modes characterized by HPLC, mass spectrometry, and gel electrophoresis. Lesion repair kinetics will be measured using cell extracts and live-cell assays monitoring GFP-tagged NER proteins. Cytotoxicity and cellular uptake will be evaluated in NER-proficient and -deficient cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel 'inverse design' paradigm for metallodrug discovery, moving from lesion function to drug structure. It will deliver new chemical tools to probe DNA repair pathways and potentially lead to next-generation platinum agents with improved therapeutic indices by evading primary repair.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the traditional drug discovery pipeline by starting from the desired, repair-resistant lesion outcome. It uniquely integrates Lippard's expertise in platinum/zinc biology and synthetic chemistry with computational lesion modeling, a combination not yet applied to this specific problem.

proposals.title: Photoactivatable Multinuclear Platinum Prodrugs for Spatiotemporally Controlled DNA Crosslinking and Cancer Cell Killing
proposals.motivation: Multinuclear platinum complexes (e.g., BBR3464) exhibit a distinct mechanism of action involving long-range DNA crosslinks, overcoming some cisplatin resistance pathways, but they still lack tumor selectivity and cause systemic toxicity. Photoactivated chemotherapy (PACT) offers precise spatial and temporal control over drug activation, minimizing off-target effects, yet most PACT agents are mononuclear and rely on singlet oxygen generation rather than direct, potent DNA crosslinking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating photocleavable protecting groups onto the bridging ligands of trinuclear platinum complexes will create inert prodrugs that, upon visible light irradiation, will release the active multinuclear scaffold, enabling spatiotemporally controlled formation of cytotoxic long-range DNA crosslinks within cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of trinuclear Pt(IV) prodrugs, where the chloride-bridging ligands are caged with ortho-nitrobenzyl (ONB) or BODIPY-based photocleavable groups, rendering the complex inert. The photophysical properties (activation wavelength, quantum yield) will be tuned by modifying the caging group. Activation kinetics, cellular uptake (using ICP-MS), and DNA binding (via atomic absorption and plasmid unwinding assays) will be measured pre- and post-irradiation with 400-500 nm light. Cytotoxicity (IC50) and the induction of synthetic lethality (combining with ATR/CHK1 inhibitors, as in Lippard's recent work on augmented synthetic lethality) will be evaluated in cisplatin-sensitive and -resistant cancer cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first class of photoactivatable, multinuclear DNA crosslinking agents, merging the potency of polynuclear platinum pharmacology with the precision of PACT.
