It will provide a novel strategy to confine the dose-limiting toxicity of these agents to irradiated tumor sites and create a platform for combining light-triggered DNA damage with targeted inhibitors of DNA damage response pathways.
proposals.novelty: While photoactivation of mononuclear Pt(IV) prodrugs is known, applying this strategy to multinuclear cores to control the formation of long-range DNA crosslinks is unprecedented. The approach uniquely leverages the distinct pharmacophore of polynuclear platinum for a spatially targeted synthetic lethality strategy.

proposals.title: Zinc-Finger-Inspired Phenanthriplatin Conjugates for Selective Targeting of Nucleosomal DNA in Aneuploid Cancers
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin and its derivatives like phenanthriplatin primarily target nuclear DNA, but their efficacy is limited by repair mechanisms and poor selectivity for higher-order chromatin structures. Pre-2026 work shows that aneuploid cancer cells exhibit altered chromatin organization and zinc homeostasis, and that synthetic zinc complexes can mediate selective release in cells. Targeting the nucleosome, the fundamental unit of chromatin, could bypass traditional repair pathways and exploit the unique epigenetic state of cancer cells.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetic platinum(IV) prodrugs, conjugated to zinc-chelating motifs inspired by native zinc-finger domains, will selectively bind and modify nucleosomal DNA over free DNA, leading to enhanced cytotoxicity in cancer cells with dysregulated zinc metabolism and aneuploidy.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of phenanthriplatin-derived Pt(IV) prodrugs tethered to zinc-chelating groups (e.g., tris(2-pyridylmethyl)amine). Using pre-2026 techniques, we will employ fluorescence-based uptake assays and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) to measure cellular zinc and platinum accumulation. Nucleosome core particles will be reconstituted in vitro to evaluate binding selectivity via electrophoretic mobility shift assays and platinum-DNA adduct mapping. Cytotoxicity and synthetic lethality will be assessed in isogenic cell lines with defined nucleotide excision repair deficiencies and engineered aneuploidy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of metallodrugs designed to engage chromatin as a target, moving beyond simple DNA adduct formation. It will provide a mechanistic link between cellular zinc biology, chromatin structure, and platinum drug efficacy, potentially identifying a novel synthetic lethal interaction for cancers with specific genomic instability.
proposals.novelty: While zinc-finger domains are known to recognize DNA structure, their mimicry to guide platinum warheads to nucleosomes is unexplored. Combining the cell-penetrating phenanthriplatin scaffold with zinc-targeting represents a novel strategy to achieve chromatin-selective damage.

proposals.title: Selective Deactivation of Phenanthriplatin Analogues by Zinc-Finger Mimetic Chelators in Healthy Tissues
proposals.motivation: Platinum anticancer drugs like cisplatin require intracellular activation, leading to systemic toxicity. The Lippard group's recent work on mobile zinc and synthetic lethality, including the development of phenanthriplatin, demonstrates the potential of non-traditional platinum agents. This proposal seeks to invert the activation paradigm to reduce off-target effects, a major limitation in chemotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: That platinum(IV) prodrugs of active phenanthriplatin analogues can be selectively reduced to their active Pt(II) form in tumor tissue, while being competitively and irreversibly deactivated in healthy tissues by high-affinity, cell-impermeable zinc-finger mimetic chelators that sequester the Pt(II) center.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Pt(IV) prodrugs derived from phenanthriplatin scaffold, varying axial ligands for tuned redox potential. Their activation will be measured in cell lysates via ICP-MS and fluorescence-based DNA binding assays. Concurrently, we will design and synthesize tridentate chelators inspired by zinc-finger domains, with high selectivity for Pt(II) over Zn(II). Deactivation kinetics will be tested in co-cultures and a microfluidic model of tissue compartments, measuring platinum uptake and cytotoxicity specifically in 'healthy' versus 'tumor' zones.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for spatially controlled drug deactivation, moving beyond prodrug activation. It will provide a new chemical strategy to enhance therapeutic windows for platinum drugs and contribute fundamental insights into the competition between Pt(II) sequestration and DNA binding in a biological context.
proposals.novelty: While prodrug activation is standard, the intentional deactivation of an already-active drug species in healthy compartments using targeted chelation is a radical departure. It uniquely combines the group's expertise in zinc biology, synthetic Pt chemistry, and cellular uptake studies to solve a core problem in metallodrug delivery.

proposals.title: Computational Fragment-Based Design of Zinc-Binding Probes for Synthetic Lethality in Aneuploid Cancers
proposals.motivation: Fragment-based drug design (FBDD) has revolutionized small-molecule discovery but is rarely applied to metal complexes, which offer unique geometries and reactivities for targeting proteins. Stephen Lippard's pre-2026 work on mobile zinc signaling, synthetic lethality with cisplatin, and zinc-catalyzed release in cells provides a strong biological foundation. This project addresses the unmet need for rational, computational tools to design metallo-fragments that exploit metal-specific interactions for novel therapeutic strategies, particularly in cancers with defined vulnerabilities like aneuploidy.
proposals.hypothesis: That computational fragment-based screening, using tailored scoring functions for metal-ligand coordination, can identify novel zinc-binding scaffolds which, when complexed, selectively disrupt protein-protein interactions or enzymatic activities in pathways synthetically lethal with chromosomal instability.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline integrating the Rosetta molecular modeling suite and AutoDock Vina, modified with custom force fields for Zn(II) coordination geometries (tetrahedral, trigonal bipyramidal). A virtual library of metallo-fragments, built from Lippard's phenanthriplatin-like cores and bidentate chelators, will be screened against target protein surfaces (e.g., from nucleotide excision repair or mitotic checkpoint complexes). Docking poses will be scored for metal coordination strength, complementarity, and predicted cellular uptake, followed by molecular dynamics simulations (AMBER) to assess complex stability. Top candidates will be synthesized and tested for zinc displacement, cellular uptake, and synthetic lethal interactions in aneuploid cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable computational methodology for metallo-fragment-based drug design, expanding the chemical space for probe and drug discovery. It will produce novel zinc-targeted chemical tools to dissect pathways of synthetic lethality, potentially leading to new strategies for augmenting chemotherapy response. The project bridges computational chemistry, inorganic synthesis, and cancer cell biology within the Lippard group's expertise.
proposals.novelty: While FBDD is mature for organic molecules, its systematic application to design metal-coordinating fragments for disrupting protein interfaces is unexplored. The approach uniquely combines computational screening for metal-ligand coordination with a biological strategy (synthetic lethality in aneuploidy) directly informed by the group's recent work.

proposals.title: Catalytic Nanoplatforms for Intracellular Pt(IV) Prodrug Activation: Leveraging Synthetic Lethality and Zinc Biology to Overcome Chemoresistance
proposals.motivation: Pt(IV) prodrugs offer a promising strategy to mitigate the systemic toxicity of platinum chemotherapeutics like cisplatin, but their clinical efficacy is limited by heterogeneous and often inefficient intracellular reduction to the active Pt(II) species. This variability contributes to chemoresistance. Pre-2026 work from the Lippard lab has established the critical roles of mobile zinc in cellular regulation and synthetic lethality approaches for enhancing platinum response, providing a unique foundation to address this activation bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering targeted nanoparticles to deliver catalysts for the intracellular reduction of Pt(IV) prodrugs will generate a robust and uniform cytotoxic Pt(II) payload, overcoming cell-to-cell variability in activation and synergizing with co-targeting strategies that exploit synthetic lethality.
proposals.method: We will synthesize lipid-polymer hybrid nanoparticles functionalized with targeting ligands (e.g., for transferrin receptor) to ensure efficient cellular uptake. The nanoparticle core will be loaded with a reduction catalyst, such as a synthetic organic redox agent or a biocompatible metal complex inspired by native zinc catalysis principles. We will employ fluorescence-based probes and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) to quantify Pt(IV) uptake and reduction kinetics in various cancer cell lines, including models with acquired cisplatin resistance. The therapeutic efficacy will be evaluated in combination with inhibitors of nucleotide excision repair or cell-cycle checkpoints, assessing synergy via clonogenic assays and measurements of aneuploidy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, catalysis-driven platform for controlled activation of platinum prodrugs directly within tumor cells. It will provide a mechanistic link between targeted catalyst delivery and the augmentation of synthetic lethality, offering a generalizable strategy to potentiate platinum-based chemotherapy and circumvent resistance mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While nanoparticle drug delivery is well-established, the use of nanoparticles as carriers for catalysts that perform a specific bio-orthogonal activation reaction (Pt(IV) reduction) inside cells is a distinct and underdeveloped approach. Integrating this catalytic concept with the lab's expertise in zinc biology and synthetic lethality creates a uniquely targeted therapeutic strategy.

proposals.title: A Pt(IV)-Superparamagnetic Iron Oxide Nanoconjugate for MRI-Guided Chemotherapy and Synthetic Lethality
proposals.motivation: A major limitation of platinum-based chemotherapy, including cisplatin and phenanthriplatin, is the inability to non-invasively monitor drug delivery and tumor uptake, which is critical for predicting and enhancing response. Pre-2026 work in the Lippard lab has focused on modulating cellular metal ion biology (e.g., zinc, calcium) and augmenting synthetic lethality to improve platinum efficacy. Integrating real-time imaging with therapeutic delivery (theranostics) could directly address this limitation and provide a platform for co-targeting strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-loading a Pt(IV) prodrug onto functionalized superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) will create an effective theranostic agent, enabling magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast for tracking tumor accumulation while the intracellular reduction of the Pt(IV) core releases an active platinum species to induce synthetic lethality in combination with DNA repair inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a Pt(IV) prodrug derivative of phenanthriplatin or cisplatin with axial carboxylate ligands for conjugation. This will be covalently attached to amine-functionalized, dextran-coated SPIONs via standard carbodiimide coupling. The nanoconjugate will be characterized for Pt loading, hydrodynamic size, and magnetic relaxivity (r2). We will assess cellular uptake in cancer cell lines using ICP-MS for platinum and iron quantification, correlating with MRI signal changes. Therapeutic efficacy and synthetic lethal interactions will be evaluated via cell viability assays (e.g., MTT) in combination with inhibitors of nucleotide excision repair (e.g., ERCC1 knockdown) or cell-cycle checkpoints (e.g., ATR inhibitors), as established in recent lab work.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel theranostic platform that directly links MRI-visible drug delivery to a therapeutic outcome, providing a tool to validate tumor targeting in vivo. It will advance the field of platinum drug delivery by integrating real-time imaging with the lab's established strategies for enhancing chemotherapy response through synthetic lethality, moving towards personalized treatment regimens.
proposals.novelty: While SPIONs and Pt(IV) prodrugs have been explored separately, their integration specifically with phenanthriplatin-derived agents for combined imaging and augmented synthetic lethality is unexplored. The novelty lies in using the MRI contrast to quantitatively correlate local drug concentration with the activation of pre-defined, targetable synthetic lethal pathways in cancer cells.

proposals.title: Dual-Targeted Zinc-Loaded Nanoparticles for Enhanced Cisplatin Delivery and Synthetic Lethality in Prostate Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite the efficacy of platinum-based drugs like cisplatin, their clinical utility is limited by systemic toxicity and intrinsic or acquired resistance. Prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) is a well-established target for prostate cancer, but tumor heterogeneity and vascular barriers can limit the uptake and efficacy of targeted monotherapies. Pre-2026 work in the Lippard lab on mobile zinc, synthetic lethality, and nanoparticle-mediated release provides a foundation for a novel combinatorial targeting strategy to overcome these delivery challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that polymeric nanoparticles co-functionalized with a PSMA-targeting ligand and a tumor vasculature-targeting peptide (e.g., RGD) will synergistically enhance tumor-specific accumulation and cellular uptake, and that the co-delivery of cisplatin with zinc ions will exploit synthetic lethality by disrupting DNA repair and cell-cycle checkpoints in cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will synthesize biodegradable PLGA-PEG nanoparticles loaded with cisplatin and a zinc-phenanthroline complex. The nanoparticle surface will be decorated with a PSMA-binding small molecule (e.g., DUPA-PEG) and a cyclic RGD peptide via controlled bioconjugation. Uptake and cytotoxicity will be evaluated in PSMA-positive (LNCaP) and PSMA-negative (PC-3) prostate cancer cells using flow cytometry and MTT assays. In vivo biodistribution and efficacy will be assessed in murine xenograft models using fluorescence imaging and tumor volume measurements, with phenanthriplatin and free cisplatin as controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel dual-targeted nanoplatform that physically enhances drug delivery to tumors and biologically augments platinum-induced synthetic lethality via zinc-mediated pathways. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how coordinated metal delivery can modulate chemotherapy response, directly extending the lab's research on zinc biology and platinum anticancer agents.
proposals.novelty: While PSMA-targeted nanoparticles exist, the combination with vascular targeting for enhanced penetration and the intentional co-delivery of zinc to create a synthetic lethal interaction with cisplatin is a novel, multi-faceted approach grounded in the lab's unique expertise in bioinorganic chemistry and cancer biology.

proposals.title: A Yeast Synthetic Lethality Screen Identifies Conserved Genetic Modifiers of Cisplatin Cytotoxicity
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin remains a cornerstone of chemotherapy, but resistance and toxicity are major clinical limitations. While nucleotide excision repair (NER) is a known resistance pathway, a comprehensive, unbiased genetic map of conserved cellular factors influencing cisplatin response is lacking. Yeast genetics offers a powerful, high-throughput system to identify such factors, which can then be validated in mammalian cells to reveal new therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic synthetic lethality screen in Saccharomyces cerevisiae will identify conserved non-essential genes that, when deleted, sensitize cells to cisplatin, revealing novel pathways that modulate platinum-drug cytotoxicity.
proposals.method: We will employ the yeast non-essential gene deletion collection in a high-throughput synthetic lethality screen, quantifying growth inhibition via robotic pinning onto cisplatin-containing media. Hits will be validated with spot assays and dose-response curves. Secondary screens will assess genetic interactions with known NER mutants (e.g., rad14Δ) to identify co-targeting opportunities, and mammalian orthologs of high-confidence hits will be tested for cisplatin uptake and sensitivity in human cancer cell lines using siRNA knockdown.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a definitive genetic interaction map for cisplatin in a model eukaryote, identifying novel conserved genes and pathways that influence platinum sensitivity. It will directly test the 'augmented synthetic lethality' concept from Lippard's recent work by finding genes that synergize with NER inhibition, providing a pipeline for translating yeast genetics into actionable mammalian cancer biology and potential combination therapies.
proposals.novelty: While yeast screens for DNA-damaging agents exist, a focused, systematic synthetic lethality screen for cisplatin, integrated with secondary analysis of NER genetic interactions and direct translation to mammalian cell models, is novel. This approach leverages conserved biology to bypass the complexity of mammalian systems for discovery.

proposals.title: Exploiting DNA Repair Deficiencies with Synthetic Lethality: Design of Zinc-Activated Platinum(IV) Prodrugs for NER-Deficient Cancers
proposals.motivation: The success of cisplatin is limited by toxicity and resistance, often linked to enhanced DNA repair. Conversely, cancers with specific DNA repair deficiencies, like Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER) defects, represent a therapeutic opportunity for synthetic lethality. Pre-2026 work by the Lippard lab on phenanthriplatin and zinc-activated prodrugs provides a unique chemical toolkit to design agents that are selectively toxic in such repair-deficient contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that platinum(IV) prodrugs, designed to be selectively activated by intracellular zinc in NER-deficient cancer cells, will induce persistent, irreparable DNA lesions, leading to augmented synthetic lethality and improved therapeutic index.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of platinum(IV) prodrugs bearing zinc-responsive, traceless release ligands, inspired by the β-cell work. Prodrug stability, zinc-dependent reduction kinetics, and DNA binding profiles will be characterized. Using isogenic cell pairs differing in NER capacity (e.g., XPA-/-), we will measure cellular uptake, cytotoxicity, and cell-cycle checkpoint activation. High-content imaging and flow cytometry will assess synthetic lethal interactions by co-targeting with ATR/Chk1 inhibitors, as previously explored.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel prodrug strategy that directly links a tumor microenvironment trigger (elevated labile zinc) to the exploitation of a tumor-specific genetic defect (NER deficiency). It will deliver new chemical entities with a clear mechanistic rationale for selective cancer cell killing, moving beyond traditional platinum agents.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its inverse design: starting from a known repair defect to engineer a prodrug activation mechanism, uniquely combining zinc-responsive chemistry with the synthetic lethality of platinum lesions in NER-deficient backgrounds, a convergence not yet explored.

proposals.title: Zinc-Dependent Coordination of Hydroxylase Activity: Defining the Role of Reductase-Regulatory Protein Interactions in Substrate Gating and Cellular Metal Homeostasis
proposals.motivation: The Lippard lab has established that mobile zinc (Zn²⁺) is a critical signaling ion, modulating processes from sensory perception to drug response. In metalloenzyme systems, such as those involving hydroxylases, substrate access is often gated by complex protein-protein interactions and metal cofactor availability, but the precise mechanisms coordinating reductase activity, regulatory protein binding, and metal ion flux to control substrate turnover remain poorly defined. Understanding this coordination is crucial for elucidating how cells integrate metal homeostasis with fundamental biochemical pathways, with potential implications for diseases of metal misregulation and for designing metallo-directed chemotherapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, zinc-sensitive interactions between a reductase and its regulatory protein partner directly govern the gating of substrate access to the associated hydroxylase active site, and that disruption of these interactions alters local zinc dynamics, pathway flux, and cellular phenotype.
proposals.method: We will employ a synthetic biology and chemical biology approach using engineered human cell lines. Specific protein-protein interactions will be probed using fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) biosensors (e.g., mTurquoise2/mNeonGreen pair) targeted to the reductase-regulatory protein interface, enabling real-time monitoring of interaction dynamics in live cells. Zinc dependence will be assessed by modulating intracellular labile Zn²⁺ pools using extracellular chelators (TPEN) and zinc ionophores (ZnPT), and by creating point mutations in putative zinc-binding motifs identified via sequence analysis and molecular docking (using HADDOCK). Phenotypic consequences of disrupting these interactions will be quantified via high-content imaging analysis of cell-cycle profiles (using DAPI and phospho-histone H3 staining) and measures of aneuploidy, linking molecular coordination to cellular outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a mechanistic model for how protein-protein interactions, modulated by zinc, coordinate enzyme complex activity. It will directly connect the lab's expertise in mobile zinc biology and chemotherapy response to fundamental questions of pathway regulation. The findings may reveal new targets for 'augmented synthetic lethality' strategies in cancer therapy by disrupting metal-dependent metabolic coordination.
proposals.novelty: While protein interactions in enzyme complexes are known, the explicit role of mobile zinc as a dynamic modulator of these interactions to gate substrate access in a hydroxylase system is unexplored. This integrates the concept of zinc as a signaling ion with precise mechanistic enzymology in a live-cell context, moving beyond static structural studies.

proposals.title: Reversible Zinc-Based Fluorescent Probes for Real-Time Imaging of Glutathione-Mediated Platinum Drug Detoxification Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Cellular glutathione (GSH) is a primary mechanism of platinum drug resistance, sequestering agents like cisplatin and phenanthriplatin into inactive metal-thiolate complexes. Current strategies to inhibit GSH synthesis, such as buthionine sulfoximine (BSO), cause broad toxicity due to sustained depletion. A method to monitor the dynamic, real-time engagement of GSH during platinum exposure is needed to identify critical, transient windows of vulnerability in the detoxification pathway, enabling temporally precise intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that labile Zn²⁺ pools, which are known to be displaced by GSH and other thiols, can serve as a reversible, real-time fluorescent readout for GSH mobilization during platinum drug treatment, and that co-administration of GSH synthesis inhibitors synchronized to peak sensor signal will selectively enhance platinum-DNA adduct formation and cell death.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize a series of turn-on fluorescent zinc sensors based on the Zinpyr and Zinspy scaffolds, tuning their Zn²⁺ affinity (Kd ~ nM to μM) and selectivity over other biological metals. These probes will be used in live-cell imaging (confocal microscopy) of cancer cell lines (including those with known cisplatin resistance) treated with cisplatin or phenanthriplatin, correlating fluorescence changes (indicating Zn²⁺ displacement) with intracellular platinum uptake (via ICP-MS) and GSH levels (via HPLC). We will then apply transient BSO pulses at times of peak sensor signal and quantify platinum-DNA adducts (via atomic absorption spectroscopy) and cell viability, comparing to continuous BSO treatment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of chemical tools for monitoring metal-thiolate biochemistry in real time, directly linking GSH dynamics to platinum drug efficacy. It will provide a mechanistic framework for 'chrono-chemotherapy,' where adjuvant therapies are timed to specific metabolic states, potentially overcoming a key resistance mechanism with reduced off-target toxicity.
proposals.novelty: While zinc sensors are established, their use as reversible reporters of GSH activity during platinum drug action is novel. The approach moves beyond static GSH measurement or permanent inhibition to a dynamic, sensor-guided strategy for augmenting synthetic lethality, leveraging Lippard's expertise in mobile zinc and platinum anticancer agents.

proposals.title: A Theranostic NIR-Zn2+ Sensor-Glutathione Inhibitor Conjugate for Image-Guided Potentiation of Cisplatin in Deep Tumors
proposals.motivation: Platinum-based chemotherapeutics like cisplatin are sequestered and inactivated by intracellular glutathione (GSH), a major resistance mechanism in deep-seated, hypoxic tumors. While GSH inhibitors exist, their efficacy is variable and difficult to monitor non-invasively in deep tissue. Pre-2026 work from the Lippard lab and others has established that GSH depletion triggers a surge in labile Zn2+, providing a potential optical biomarker for therapeutic effect.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a theranostic agent combining a near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent Zn2+ sensor with a GSH inhibitor will enable real-time, deep-tissue imaging of GSH inhibition efficacy, and that this visual feedback can guide dosing to maximally sensitize resistant tumors to cisplatin by preventing platinum sequestration.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a conjugate linking a potent, cell-permeable GSH inhibitor (e.g., buthionine sulfoximine, BSO, or a more potent analog) to a NIR-excitable, turn-on Zn2+ sensor based on a dicyanoisophorone or aza-BODIPY fluorophore scaffold, optimized for >700 nm emission. The conjugate's ability to deplete GSH and report via Zn2+ fluorescence will be validated in 2D and 3D spheroid cultures of cisplatin-resistant cancer cells using fluorescence microscopy and ICP-MS. In vivo efficacy and imaging guidance will be tested in murine models with deep-seated tumors, using NIR fluorescence tomography to monitor Zn2+ surges and guide subsequent cisplatin administration, with platinum-DNA adduct formation quantified via atomic absorption spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-in-class theranostic tool that directly links a therapeutic action (GSH inhibition) to a deep-tissue-readable optical signal. It will establish a methodology for image-guided chemotherapy potentiation and provide fundamental insights into the spatial and temporal dynamics of the GSH-Zn2+-Pt interaction network in tumors.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of a NIR Zn2+ sensor with a GSH inhibitor creates a single chemical entity for simultaneous intervention and deep-tissue feedback, a strategy distinct from co-administering separate agents. Using Zn2+ as a real-time, in vivo biomarker for GSH inhibition efficacy to guide platinum dosing is a novel conceptual and practical advance.

proposals.title: Mapping Spatiotemporal Zinc Fluxes to Decipher the Dichotomy of Platinum-Induced Autophagy in Cancer Cells
proposals.motivation: Platinum-based chemotherapeutics like cisplatin induce autophagy, but its role as a pro-survival resistance mechanism or a pro-death pathway remains context-dependent and poorly understood, limiting therapeutic exploitation. Concurrently, our lab and others have established that mobile zinc (Zn²⁺) is a critical signaling ion, with its dysregulation implicated in stress responses and cell death. This proposal addresses the critical gap in understanding how dynamic metal ion biology directly interfaces with the autophagic machinery in the context of chemotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that platinum drug treatment triggers specific, spatiotemporally defined zinc signals, and that the amplitude, location, and duration of these zinc fluxes determine whether the ensuing autophagy promotes cell survival or commits the cell to death.
proposals.method: We will use live-cell, multi-parameter confocal microscopy in non-small cell lung cancer (A549) and ovarian cancer (A2780) cells. Cells will be co-loaded with the Zn²⁺ sensor Zinpyr-1 (or Zinpyr-4 for improved specificity), the autophagic flux reporter mRFP-GFP-LC3, and cell death markers (e.g., Annexin V, propidium iodide). High-content image analysis will be performed using CellProfiler to quantify cytosolic vs. vesicular zinc signals, autophagosome number and acidification, and cell fate over time following treatment with cisplatin and its more potent analog, phenanthriplatin. Synthetic lethality approaches, such as co-treatment with ATR inhibitors (pre-2026 standard), will be used to modulate DNA damage response and probe its connection to zinc release.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between trace metal ion dynamics and a major cell fate decision pathway in chemotherapy. It will provide the first 'zinc signature' maps predictive of autophagic cell death versus survival. The findings could reveal novel co-targeting strategies, such as manipulating zinc transporters or chelators, to convert cytoprotective autophagy into a lethal event, thereby augmenting platinum efficacy.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond the binary debate on autophagy's function by introducing a specific, measurable metal ion as a decisive regulatory variable. It uniquely applies the Lippard lab's expertise in zinc biology and platinum drug mechanisms to solve a persistent problem in cancer cell biology, using spatiotemporal imaging to connect metal flux with organelle-specific outcomes.

proposals.title: Ratiometric Fluorescent Sensors for Labile Cu(I) and Fe(II) to Decipher Their Role in Platinum-Induced Autophagic Fate Switching
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin and its analogues remain frontline chemotherapeutics, but resistance is a major clinical obstacle. Autophagy is a critical, yet double-edged, cellular response to platinum drugs, promoting either survival or death, but the mechanisms controlling this fate are unclear. Pre-2026 work from the Lippard lab and others has established that mobile zinc and calcium are key signaling agents, and that redox-active metals like copper and iron can influence drug response and oxidative stress pathways, suggesting they may be pivotal regulators of autophagic outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cisplatin treatment perturbs labile pools of redox-active Cu(I) and Fe(II), and that these metal fluxes act as a critical switch, converting autophagy from a pro-survival mechanism to a cytotoxic process. The specific subcellular localization and magnitude of the metal flux will determine the autophagic fate.
proposals.method: We will synthesize selective, ratiometric fluorescein-based Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) sensors for labile Cu+ and Fe2+, building on pre-2026 designs for Zn2+ sensors. Sensor specificity will be validated in vitro using metal buffers and competing ions. In live cancer cells, we will use confocal microscopy to perform real-time, compartment-specific (e.g., lysosomal, mitochondrial) tracking of Cu+/Fe2+ flux following treatment with cisplatin and phenanthriplatin. This data will be correlated temporally and spatially with established fluorescent markers of autophagic flux (e.g., LC3-RFP/mCherry) and cell death, using image analysis algorithms for colocalization and intensity quantification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first chemical tools for visualizing labile Cu(I) and Fe(II) dynamics during chemotherapy, establishing a direct, causal link between metal homeostasis and autophagic cell fate. It will create a novel mechanistic framework for understanding platinum-induced stress signaling and identify specific metal-dependent processes as potential new targets for therapeutic intervention to overcome chemoresistance.
proposals.novelty: While the role of Zn2+ in signaling is established, directly probing the dynamics of the more challenging redox-active Cu+ and Fe2+ pools in the context of autophagy is unprecedented. Fusing this precise metal detection with the study of autophagic fate switching represents a novel chemical biology approach to a central problem in cancer pharmacology.

proposals.title: Proteomic Profiling of the Cisplatin Adduct Interactome: Identifying Novel Recognition Factors Beyond NER
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin's efficacy is limited by inherent and acquired resistance, which is often linked to the cellular response to platinum-DNA lesions. While nucleotide excision repair (NER) proteins are known to process these adducts, the full spectrum of proteins that initially recognize and bind to them to signal downstream events—such as cell-cycle arrest, apoptosis, or tolerance—remains incompletely defined. A comprehensive identification of these recognition factors is crucial for understanding resistance mechanisms and for developing co-targeting strategies, as suggested by recent work on synthetic lethality with NER and checkpoint inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific set of DNA damage response (DDR) and chromatin-associated proteins, beyond canonical NER factors, directly and selectively recognize cisplatin-DNA adducts, and that modulating these proteins will alter cellular sensitivity to platinum agents.
proposals.method: We will employ a synthetic biology and proteomics approach. First, we will generate defined, site-specific 1,2-d(GpG) cisplatin intrastrand crosslinks within a biotinylated, double-stranded DNA oligonucleotide using established synthetic methods. This bait will be incubated with nuclear extracts from relevant cell lines (e.g., A549, isogenic NER-proficient/deficient pairs). Proteins bound to the platinated DNA will be isolated via streptavidin pull-down and identified by quantitative liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), comparing against unplatinated controls. Candidate proteins will be validated using CRISPRi knockdowns followed by assays for cisplatin uptake, cell viability (MTT), and DNA damage signaling (γ-H2AX, pChk1).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, proteome-wide map of proteins that directly engage with the major cisplatin-DNA lesion. It will identify novel, lesion-specific recognition factors that could serve as biomarkers for patient response or as new therapeutic targets to augment synthetic lethality approaches. The findings will bridge the molecular recognition event to downstream cellular phenotypes of cell death and aneuploidy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on NER proteins, this proposal systematically searches for the entire recognition complex, including potential chromatin remodelers and non-canonical DDR factors, using a precisely defined, synthetic lesion. This approach moves beyond correlative studies to establish direct physical interactions.

proposals.title: Decoding the Crosstalk Between ATR/ATM, MAPK, and p53 Signaling in Cellular Responses to Platinum-Based Chemotherapeutics
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin and its analogs, like phenanthriplatin, exert their cytotoxic effects primarily by forming DNA adducts, triggering complex damage response networks involving ATR/ATM, MAPK, and p53 pathways. Pre-2026 understanding indicates these pathways do not operate in isolation, but their precise temporal interplay and crosstalk, which likely dictates cell fate decisions between repair, cell-cycle arrest, and apoptosis, remain poorly mapped. Characterizing this signaling network is critical for understanding mechanisms of resistance and for designing rational co-targeting strategies, such as synthetic lethality with DNA repair inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that platinum-induced DNA damage initiates a defined, temporally regulated cascade of crosstalk between the ATR/ATM, MAPK, and p53 pathways, and that modulating intracellular zinc levels, a known cellular signaling modulator, will selectively rewire this network to enhance platinum sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will treat a panel of cancer cell lines (including isogenic pairs with defined DNA repair deficiencies) with cisplatin and phenanthriplatin, monitoring pathway activation via high-content, multiplexed immunofluorescence (using antibodies for p-ATR/ATM, p-Chk1/2, p-p38/JNK/ERK, and p53/p21) over a 72-hour time course. Signaling network dynamics will be modeled using partial least squares regression and Bayesian network inference to map causal interactions. Parallel experiments will employ zinc chelators (TPEN) and zinc ionophores to assess the role of mobile zinc in modulating this crosstalk, linking to prior work on zinc in sensory perception and β-cell function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, dynamic map of DNA damage-induced signaling crosstalk, directly linking platinum adduct formation to downstream cell fate decisions. It will establish a novel role for mobile zinc as a tunable modulator of this network, offering a new biochemical lever to augment platinum efficacy. The findings will inform the development of next-generation combination therapies that co-target specific nodes within this network to overcome resistance.
proposals.novelty: While individual pathways are well-studied, the integrated, temporal modeling of their crosstalk in response to different platinum agents is novel. Combining this systems-level signaling analysis with manipulation of mobile zinc biology creates a unique, non-obvious intersection of Lippard's expertise in platinum chemistry, zinc biology, and synthetic lethality.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Visualization of Nucleotide Excision Repair Protein Dynamics at Site-Specific Platinum-DNA Lesions
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin's efficacy and toxicity are governed by the cellular response to its DNA adducts, primarily processed by the nucleotide excision repair (NER) pathway. Pre-2026 knowledge indicates that lesion recognition and repair initiation are critical but poorly understood dynamic processes. Directly observing the real-time binding and exchange of NER proteins at individual lesions would reveal the kinetic principles underlying repair efficiency and synthetic lethality with cell-cycle checkpoints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the binding kinetics and stoichiometry of NER recognition complexes (e.g., XPC-RAD23B, TFIIH) at defined platinum-DNA lesions are heterogeneous and modulated by the lesion's structural distortion and the cellular zinc status, which is known to influence protein-DNA interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ single-molecule total internal reflection fluorescence (smTIRF) microscopy. Site-specific 1,2-d(GpG) cisplatin or phenanthriplatin intrastrand crosslinks will be incorporated into DNA constructs and tethered to a passivated microscope slide. Key NER proteins (XPC, TFIIH subunits) will be labeled with organic fluorophores (e.g., Cy3, Alexa647) via maleimide chemistry. Binding events, dwell times, and co-localization will be tracked in real time in buffers with controlled Zn²⁺ levels. Data will be analyzed using custom single-particle tracking and step-finding algorithms (e.g., hidden Markov modeling) to extract kinetic parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative map of the protein assembly dynamics at the fundamental platinum-DNA lesion. It will establish how lesion structure (cisplatin vs. phenanthriplatin) and zinc availability impact repair initiation kinetics, offering a mechanistic basis for designing augmented synthetic lethality strategies that co-target NER and cell-cycle checkpoints.
proposals.novelty: While bulk studies of NER exist, applying single-molecule imaging to precisely engineered platinum-DNA lesions is novel. Integrating this with the modulation by zinc, a key biological modulator in Lippard's recent work, creates a unique link between metal homeostasis and the real-time mechanics of DNA damage recognition.

proposals.title: Mapping Platinum-Induced 3D Genome Reorganization and Its Role in Chemotherapy Response and Aneuploidy
proposals.motivation: Cisplatin and its analogs exert cytotoxicity primarily by forming DNA crosslinks, but the cellular response involves complex, genome-wide changes in transcription and DNA repair. Pre-2026 work has established that DNA damage can alter local chromatin architecture and that mobile zinc modulates cellular stress responses. However, the global, three-dimensional reorganization of the genome following platinum damage and its functional consequences for drug response, synthetic lethality, and the emergence of aneuploidy remain largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that platinum-DNA damage induces specific, large-scale alterations in chromatin topology, compartmentalization, and looping, and that these 3D genome changes are functionally linked to cell fate decisions, the efficacy of synthetic lethal combinations, and the genomic instability that can lead to aneuploidy.
proposals.method: We will apply in situ Hi-C to human cancer cell lines treated with cisplatin and the monofunctional analog phenanthriplatin, comparing them to untreated controls. Cells will be harvested at key time points post-treatment, corresponding to initial damage recognition, repair activation, and cell-cycle checkpoint engagement. Hi-C libraries will be sequenced deeply, and data will be analyzed using established pre-2026 pipelines (e.g., Juicer, HiC-Pro) to identify changes in topologically associating domains (TADs), A/B compartments, and specific chromatin loops. These spatial maps will be integrated with orthogonal data on platinum uptake, transcriptional changes, and DNA repair protein recruitment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, three-dimensional view of genome architecture remodeling by platinum chemotherapeutics. It will identify specific spatial genomic vulnerabilities that could be co-targeted to augment synthetic lethality strategies. Furthermore, it will reveal how platinum-induced DNA damage may disrupt higher-order chromatin structures to promote the chromosomal mis-segregation and aneuploidy observed in treatment-resistant cells.
proposals.novelty: While chromatin studies in the Lippard lab have focused on zinc biology and nucleotide excision repair, applying high-resolution chromatin conformation capture to platinum damage is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond local DNA adduct biochemistry to a systems-level understanding of how genome folding influences chemotherapy outcomes, directly connecting 3D genome maps to the phenomena of synthetic lethality and aneuploidy.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Biological Evaluation of Bulky, Monofunctional Platinum-Phenanthriplatin Hybrids Designed to Evade Nucleotide Excision Repair
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of cisplatin and related chemotherapeutics is limited by cellular DNA repair, particularly nucleotide excision repair (NER), which recognizes and removes platinum-DNA adducts. Recent work on phenanthriplatin, a monofunctional platinum(IV) complex, demonstrates that bulky, transcription-inhibiting adducts can exhibit a distinct cytotoxic profile, but their recognition by NER remains a vulnerability. Engineering platinum lesions that are poorly detected by NER could overcome a primary resistance mechanism and enhance therapeutic potency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that platinum complexes bearing sterically bulky, aromatic amine ligands will form DNA adducts that structurally mimic natural base perturbations poorly recognized by the XPC-RAD23B initiator of NER, thereby increasing adduct persistence and cytotoxicity, especially in repair-proficient cancers.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of monofunctional platinum(II) and platinum(IV) complexes incorporating extended aromatic amine ligands (e.g., phenanthridine derivatives, bulky pyridines) inspired by phenanthriplatin's scaffold. Adduct formation and structure will be characterized by platination assays on defined oligonucleotides and analyzed via HPLC and ESI-MS. NER evasion will be quantified using in vitro excision assays with purified XPC-RAD23B and full NER machinery, and correlated with cellular uptake (via ICP-MS) and cytotoxicity in paired NER-proficient and -deficient cell lines. Synthetic lethality with ATR/CHK1 checkpoint inhibitors will be tested to augment response.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-activity relationship for NER evasion by platinum adducts, providing a new design principle for next-generation platinum drugs. It will deliver novel chemical tools to probe the molecular determinants of DNA damage recognition and potentially identify leads with improved activity against tumors with intact DNA repair pathways.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focused on repairing or tolerating platinum damage, this proposal directly targets the evasion of initial damage recognition, a less explored strategy. Combining the mechanistic insights from monofunctional phenanthriplatin with a deliberate focus on steric blockade of NER sensor proteins represents a novel chemical biology approach to platinum drug design.

ideas.title: Real-time, in-cellulo sensors for metal-based drug detoxification to guide combination therapy
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the concepts by developing fluorescein-PET-based sensors specifically for the redox-active metal cations (e.g., Cu+, Fe2+) that are critical cofactors for cellular detoxification machinery, such as metallothioneins and certain glutathione-associated enzymes. These sensors would be used to monitor the real-time intracellular flux of these metals in response to platinum chemotherapy, directly reporting on the activation state of the detoxification pathways targeted for inhibition. This allows researchers to not only apply detoxification inhibitors but to optimally time their administration based on the sensor signal, maximizing platinum-DNA adduct formation. Consequently, the platform moves beyond static inhibition to a dynamic, feedback-controlled strategy that personalizes combination therapy to a tumor's real-time metabolic response.

ideas.title: Co-opt bacterial toxin-antitoxin systems to trigger apoptosis in platinum-treated cancer cells
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in microbiology: how bacteria use toxin-antitoxin systems to couple a specific stress signal to a lethal response. The analogous solution involves a labile antitoxin that is degraded under stress, freeing a stable toxin to act. Adapted to cancer research, the proposal is to develop co-treatments that function as a synthetic, cancer cell-specific TA system. The 'antitoxin' would be a protector molecule or pathway inhibitor that is selectively destabilized or inactivated by the biochemical environment of a cell containing platinum-DNA adducts (the stress signal). This would unleash a co-administered 'toxin'—a potent direct activator of apoptosis—thereby specifically lowering the apoptosis threshold in the targeted, therapy-resistant cancer cells while sparing healthy cells lacking the platinum-adduct signal.

ideas.title: Dynamic Glutathione Sensors for Real-Time Monitoring of Platinum Drug Detoxification
ideas.description: This research direction develops sensors for labile Zn2+ pools that are dynamically displaced by cellular glutathione (GSH) during platinum drug exposure. Instead of using high-affinity, irreversible inhibitors that blunt the entire GSH system, these reversible sensors act as real-time reporters of GSH mobilization and metal-thiolate pool fluctuations. This allows for the identification of critical time windows where the detoxification capacity is maximally engaged but not yet exhausted. Targeted, transient inhibition of GSH synthesis or utilization can then be applied synchronously with these peaks to selectively prevent platinum sequestration, dramatically increasing the formation of lethal DNA adducts while minimizing off-target toxicity from sustained GSH depletion.

ideas.title: Exploiting Hyperactive DNA Repair as a Source of Synthetic Lethality
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that increased DNA repair is always pro-resistance by proposing that hyperactive repair could cause genomic instability and sensitize cells to other agents. Drawing an analogy from structural engineering, where over-reinforcing a brittle material creates new, catastrophic failure modes under specific stresses, we adapt this solution. The adapted research would systematically test secondary agents (e.g., ATR inhibitors, nucleoside analogs, or ROS-inducing compounds) on cells with engineered hyperactive repair pathways (like overexpressed ERCC1 or POLB) to identify synthetic lethal interactions. The goal is to map the specific 'stress conditions' under which the rigid, over-engineered repair system collapses, turning a presumed resistance mechanism into a therapeutic vulnerability.

ideas.title: Near-IR Zn2+ Sensor-Guided Inhibition of Glutathione to Potentiate Platinum Therapy in Deep Tumors
ideas.description: This project develops a combined therapeutic and deep-tissue imaging strategy by fusing a near-infrared (NIR) excitable zinc ion (Zn2+) sensor with a glutathione (GSH) inhibitor. The NIR sensor allows real-time, high-penetration imaging of intracellular Zn2+ dynamics, which serve as a direct biomarker for GSH inhibition efficacy, as GSH depletion leads to a labile Zn2+ surge. This visual feedback, obtainable from deep tissues, guides the optimal timing and dosing of the GSH inhibitor to maximally prevent platinum drug sequestration. Consequently, the approach simultaneously enables non-invasive monitoring of therapeutic response and significantly enhances platinum-DNA adduct formation in otherwise treatment-resistant deep-seated tumors.

ideas.title: A Rationetric FRET Sensor for Real-Time, Subcellular Monitoring of Zn2+ Flux During DNA Repair-Induced Genomic Instability
ideas.description: This project fuses the concepts by developing a rationetric FRET-based Zn2+ sensor, built on the Zinpyr scaffold, to investigate the role of zinc ion flux in the paradoxical effects of hyperactive DNA repair. The sensor will be used to monitor spatiotemporal changes in labile Zn2+ pools within the nucleus and cytoplasm of cells engineered for hyperactive DNA repair, testing whether repair hyperactivity dysregulates zinc homeostasis. This zinc dysregulation could then be linked to increased genomic instability and sensitization to other agents, providing a direct, mechanistic readout not possible with either idea alone. The approach allows for correlating real-time zinc dynamics with markers of DNA damage and cell fate following genotoxic stress.

ideas.title: Longitudinal CRISPR screens in engineered tumor microenvironments to map adaptive platinum resistance pathways
ideas.description: This approach employs genome-wide CRISPR knockout screens not in monolayer cells, but in patient-derived tumor organoids co-cultured with stromal and immune cells to model the tumor microenvironment. Screens are performed longitudinally, subjecting these complex cultures to repeated cycles of platinum chemotherapy and recovery to mimic clinical treatment schedules. This design identifies genes essential for surviving therapy specifically within a dynamic, heterogeneous context, revealing resistance mechanisms driven by tumor-stroma crosstalk and adaptive stress responses. Functional validation then prioritizes targets whose inhibition re-sensitizes tumors specifically under microenvironmental pressures.

ideas.title: Sensors for labile metal pools to probe the metal-dependent switch from pro-survival to cytotoxic autophagy
ideas.description: This project develops selective, ratiometric fluorescein-PET sensors for labile Cu+ and Fe2+ pools to investigate their role in modulating autophagy during platinum chemotherapy. The core hypothesis is that these redox-active metals are critical determinants that switch autophagy from a pro-survival to a pro-death pathway following platinum exposure. By enabling real-time, compartment-specific tracking of metal flux alongside autophagic markers, the research will establish a causal link between metal homeostasis and autophagic fate. This approach provides a novel chemical toolset to decipher metal-dependent signaling in cell death decisions and could identify new targets to overcome chemoresistance.

ideas.title: Spatially and Clonally Resolved Mapping of Resistance Mechanism Co-occurrence and Exclusivity in Solid Tumors
ideas.description: This project investigates whether resistance mechanisms are mutually exclusive or co-occurring at the level of individual tumor subclones and spatial regions within a single tumor. We will perform multi-region sampling from treatment-naïve and post-relapse solid tumors, followed by single-cell RNA sequencing and targeted DNA sequencing to define subclonal architecture and resistance pathway activation. These subpopulations will be isolated and subjected to parallel high-throughput functional drug screens to profile their distinct sensitivity patterns. The integrated analysis will determine if multiple resistance pathways operate simultaneously within the same malignant cell, within different cells of the same subclone, or are segregated into entirely different subclones, providing a definitive, high-resolution answer to the original question.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal Zinc Dynamics as a Determinant of Platinum-Induced Autophagic Cell Fate
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the hypothesis that specific, spatiotemporally defined zinc signals, visualized using Zinpyr sensors, determine whether platinum-induced autophagy becomes a pro-survival or pro-death pathway. By performing live-cell imaging of zinc pools concurrently with autophagic flux and cell death markers in cancer cells treated with platinum drugs, the study aims to identify distinct zinc signatures that correlate with autophagic cell death. This approach moves beyond the binary debate on autophagy's role by introducing a trace metal ion as a critical regulatory variable, potentially revealing novel therapeutic targets to shift autophagy from a resistance mechanism to a death pathway.

ideas.title: Autophagy as a Bistable Switch: Adapting Ecological Tipping Point Models to Explain Platinum-Induced Autophagic Cell Death
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that autophagy is solely a cell survival mechanism by seeking to explain contexts where platinum-induced autophagy promotes cell death. It draws an analogy from ecology and systems theory, where a single process (like nutrient cycling) can either stabilize or collapse an ecosystem based on intensity and context, modeled through bistable systems and tipping points. The analogous solution involves identifying the critical thresholds and contextual variables that determine the system's functional outcome. Adapted to cell biology, this frames autophagy as a bistable system. The research would systematically map contextual parameters—such as platinum dose, exposure kinetics, concurrent stressors (ER stress, ROS), and metabolic state—to identify the critical combinatorial thresholds that tip autophagy's function from pro-survival to pro-death. This approach moves beyond a binary view of autophagy to a systems-based model predicting its role based on quantifiable cellular conditions.

ideas.title: Tumor Resistance as a Redundant Network: Mapping Co-occurring and Mutually Exclusive Pathways
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a tumor relies on a single dominant resistance pathway by investigating if multiple pathways are employed simultaneously. Drawing an analogy from ecology's resolution of species coexistence, we adapt the concepts of competitive exclusion and niche partitioning. The adapted research involves mapping the 'ecological network' of resistance mechanisms (e.g., drug efflux, target mutation, bypass signaling, epigenetic adaptation) within a tumor population using single-cell multi-omics and functional screens. The goal is to systematically classify pairs or groups of mechanisms as either mutually exclusive (where one precludes the other due to high fitness cost or functional redundancy) or co-occurring (where they operate in complementary niches, providing robust, multi-layered resistance). This framework will reveal the combinatorial rules of resistance evolution, predicting which pathway combinations are stable and which are unstable, ultimately informing strategies for combination therapies that target co-occurring networks or exploit mutual exclusivities.

ideas.title: Solve apo-sensor structures using cryo-EM and computational refinement from structural biology
ideas.description: The original idea aims to determine X-ray structures of apo-Zinpyr forms to understand conformational changes upon metal binding, but these flexible, unbound states may be inherently difficult to crystallize. Drawing an analogy from structural biology, specifically the study of membrane proteins, where hybrid methods are standard for flexible systems. In that field, cryo-EM provides low-resolution envelopes of conformational states, which are then combined with computational flexible fitting and molecular dynamics using known high-resolution domain structures. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to employ single-particle cryo-EM to capture the heterogeneous conformations of apo-Zinpyr sensors. These experimental maps will then serve as scaffolds for computational refinement and molecular dynamics simulations, using the rigid, metal-bound crystal structures as a reference, thereby solving the conformational ensemble of the metal-free state and elucidating the binding-induced structural mechanism.

ideas.title: Adapting Radio-Tracer Imaging Principles to Map Zinpyr Sensor Compartmentalization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from nuclear medicine imaging, where tracer localization is solved via detection and reconstruction of a specific signal. The analogous problem is determining where a biologically active compound localizes in a complex 3D system. In PET, a radiolabeled tracer is introduced, and its accumulation is mapped by detecting emitted radiation. We adapt this to cell biology by treating the Zinpyr zinc sensor as a fluorescent tracer. The solution is to use high-resolution fluorescence microscopy (the detection system) in conjunction with definitive organelle markers (e.g., MitoTracker, LysoTracker) to collect spatial signal data. Advanced image analysis, including pixel-intensity correlation and 3D reconstruction algorithms, will then be used to objectively map and quantify the sensor's accumulation within specific organelles like mitochondria or vesicles, providing a rigorous, quantitative localization profile.

ideas.title: A Multi-Fidelity Computational Pipeline with Experimental Validation for Robust Zn2+/Cd2+ Selectivity Design
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates a robust pipeline for designing selective Zn2+ binders. It first uses high-level quantum chemical benchmarks to identify the most accurate DFT methods for modeling the subtle electronic differences between Zn2+ and Cd2+. These calibrated methods are then used in free energy calculations that explicitly account for solvation, dynamics, and entropic contributions to binding. Finally, the top predicted ligand structures are synthesized and tested experimentally, with the results fed back to refine the computational models, ensuring predictive reliability and practical utility.

ideas.title: Cd²⁺-Selective Zn²⁺ Sensor via Integrated Receptor Tuning and Kinetic Sieving on a BODIPY Platform
ideas.description: This redesigned approach constructs a Zn²⁺ sensor on a bright, photostable BODIPY core but directly confronts the Cd²⁺ interference problem through a dual-component design. The BODIPY is functionalized with a receptor ligand specifically optimized for thermodynamic selectivity for Zn²⁺ over Cd²⁺ via donor atom choice and backbone rigidity. Furthermore, the sensor molecule is encapsulated within or attached to a nanostructured material that acts as a kinetic sieve, exploiting differences in hydration shell dynamics and ionic radius to further slow Cd²⁺ access relative to Zn²⁺. This combined thermodynamic and kinetic discrimination creates a robust sensor with dramatically improved practical selectivity in complex environments where both ions may be present.

papers.abstract: DNA is believed to be the primary target for many metal-based drugs. For example, platinum-based anticancer drugs can form specific lesions on DNA that induce apoptosis.
